entry.title: What’s New in the SAP Cloud ERP Private 2025 FPS1 release
entry.content:
The SAP Cloud ERP Private 2025 FPS1 release brings a new level of intelligence to everyday business work by expanding how AI supports users across various LoBs. At the core of this update is Joule and SAP’s agentic AI framework, where specialized agents and conversational capabilities work together to guide users through tasks, surface the right information, and provide recommendations in the moment of need. Instead of navigating complex transactions or piecing together data from multiple systems, employees can rely on natural‑language interactions to move through their processes faster and with greater confidence. This release also delivers embedded AI features that automate checks, streamline validations, and support operational decisions with real‑time insights. The overview chart below brings together all key innovations delivered with the SAP Cloud ERP Private and SAP Cloud ERP 2025 FPS01 Release, highlighting how AI, Joule capabilities, and embedded intelligence are supporting users across every line of business.

Altogether, these innovations help organizations reduce manual effort, strengthen transparency, and keep their operations running more smoothly, while allowing users to focus on the work that truly drives business outcomes.

Agentic use cases

Finance:

Mastering cash flow with the Cash Management Agent*

The Cash Management Agent supports finance teams by automating tasks that usually drain time, checking bank statement completeness, validating opening balances, and preparing cash‑flow forecasts. Many organizations struggle with fragmented financial data and delayed insights, which makes it difficult to spot potential cash shortages or opportunities to use surpluses effectively. By relying on Joule and Joule Agents to run these daily checks, calculations, and summaries, teams can shift from manual reconciliation to confident, data‑driven decisions. This automation improves accuracy and frees cash managers to focus on strategic liquidity planning instead of routine monitoring. In the end, it strengthens daily cash‑position visibility and helps organizations manage their cash resources more efficiently.

Learn more by accessing the SAP Help Portal.

* This AI offering is currently in Beta and available at no cost. Pricing will be communicated once the AI offering becomes generally available. A separate subscription is required for use or testing. Learn more.

 

R&D:

Streamlined engineering changes with the change record management*

Engineering teams often lose momentum when a simple product or process change turns into a maze of documentation, reviews, and approvals. Coordinators and engineers must manually create change records and shepherd them through the process, which can delay decisions and slow time‑to‑market. The Change Record Management Agent uses SAP Joule to assist with creating complete and consistent change records and orchestrating the necessary workflow steps. By reducing manual effort and guiding teams through the process, it helps engineering organizations keep changes moving without administrative bottlenecks. This leads to a faster, more efficient change‑management cycle and ultimately supports quicker delivery of updates and innovations.


 Use our SAP Help page to learn more.

 

Joule capabilities

Please note that certain Joule features described in this blog may not yet be available in your system and are planned for release as part of the upcoming Joule release cycle scheduled for March/April 2026.

Finance:

Joule for invoicing specialists to enable more-seamless navigation and transactional actions within billing documents

Invoicing specialists often spend far too much time clicking through multiple screens just to find the right convergent invoicing app or complete a billing‑related task. When workloads are high, even simple actions, like navigating to a billing document or making a small update, can slow the process and impact how quickly customers receive clear and accurate invoices. With expanded Joule capabilities, specialists can move directly to the right SAP Fiori apps and perform transactional actions through natural‑language prompts, without hunting for the correct tile or transaction. This conversational support reduces manual effort and helps teams resolve billing tasks more efficiently, leading to quicker processing and a better customer experience. Over time, this can even help reduce days sales outstanding by keeping billing workflows running smoothly and consistently.

 

AI-assisted accrual object creation

Preparing manual accruals often requires finance teams to gather fragmented information, apply judgment, and cross‑check historical patterns, all of which can be time‑consuming and inconsistent across entities. When deadlines are tight, variations in how preparers estimate amounts or select accounts can increase the risk of missed accruals or adjustments later in the audit process. With AI‑assisted accrual creation in Joule, users can describe their scenario in natural language and instantly receive a proposed accrual object, including category, amount, life term, and relevant GL accounts. They can refine any attribute, and Joule adapts its suggestions in real time based on the edits, gradually improving proposal quality through continued use. This guided, conversational approach streamlines preparation, increases consistency, and helps finance teams create more accurate accruals with far less effort.

 

 

Sales:

Staying ahead with AI-assisted detecting of expiring prices using Joule 

Pricing specialists often spend valuable time hunting through condition records to find prices that are about to expire, a task that becomes even more demanding when large volumes of data and tight sales timelines are involved. Missed expirations can lead to outdated pricing in customer quotes, last‑minute firefighting, and unnecessary manual corrections. With Joule detecting expiring prices, displaying relevant condition records, and even supporting validity extensions and mass changes, specialists can handle follow‑up activities with far less effort. This reduces repetitive work, minimizes human error, and keeps pricing information accurate and up to date across the system. Ultimately, it enables teams to manage pricing proactively, saving both time and operational cost.

 

 

Use our SAP Help page to learn more.

 

AI-assisted fetching and listing of open sales documents

Internal sales reps often need to scan through multiple criteria in VA05 just to find the right open sales orders, which can slow down workflows when customers are waiting for updates. Searching by document type, dates, materials, or organizational data requires navigating several fields and filters, making the process repetitive and time‑consuming. With AI‑assisted retrieval in Joule, reps can simply ask for open sales documents, and the system instantly displays orders filtered by attributes such as customer, material, creation date, or sales area. This conversational approach reduces the manual effort needed to locate the right information and helps reps respond faster and more accurately. The result is a more efficient sales process and smoother daily operations for the team.

 Consult our SAP Help Page for more information.

AI-assisted sales quotation changes using Joule 

Updating sales quotations can be surprisingly time‑consuming, especially when internal sales reps need to adjust quantities, refine pricing, change delivery dates, or capture customer‑specific references across multiple line items. These routine updates often involve navigating several fields in VA22, increasing the chance of small mistakes that can slow down the sales cycle. With AI‑assisted quotation changes in Joule, reps can make these adjustments through simple natural‑language requests, whether they need to update a quantity, modify a price via condition type, set delivery dates, or record reasons for rejection. This conversational flow speeds up the process and reduces manual effort, helping teams respond to customer requests more quickly and accurately. The result is a smoother quotation‑management experience that keeps sales moving with less friction.

 

Service:

Searching and navigating to service contracts with Joule

Service agents often spend more time searching for the right service contract than actually solving the customer’s issue, especially when navigating by status, dates, or complex contract structures. These delays can disrupt workflows and make it harder to move quickly from one task to the next. With Joule assisting through natural‑language prompts, agents can instantly search for service contracts, open details, complete releases, or jump directly to the relevant SAP Fiori functionality. This reduces unnecessary clicks and helps users focus on completing service‑related tasks instead of trying to find them. As a result, teams can deliver faster responses and manage service contracts with more confidence and less effort.

 

 

Joule for transferring in-house service objects

Transferring an in‑house service object between plants or storage locations often requires navigating several steps in the system, from creating the outbound delivery to recording the inbound receipt. These tasks can slow down technicians and coordinators who simply want to keep service operations moving. With Joule guiding the process through conversational prompts, users can initiate the transfer, add the target plant and storage location, and create the necessary deliveries without searching for the right transaction. This reduces friction in daily logistics and helps teams complete handovers more reliably. The conversational flow not only speeds up execution but also makes the transfer process easier for occasional users who don’t perform it every day.

 Consult our SAP Help Page for more information.

 

Supply Chain:

Enabling Joule to drive operational efficiency in outbound processes in SAP Extended Warehouse Management

Warehouse teams often juggle multiple screens just to track outbound deliveries, check warehouse tasks, or confirm the status of orders—slowing down operations in environments where every minute counts. As outbound volumes grow and customer expectations tighten, the effort required to manually search, summarize, and update records can become a real bottleneck. With Joule integrated into SAP EWM, users can search for outbound delivery orders, review warehouse tasks, or even create and confirm warehouse orders simply by asking in natural language. This gives frontline teams instant insights and reduces the time spent navigating complex menus or transactions. The result is faster decision‑making, smoother outbound processing, and a more intuitive experience that helps warehouses keep goods moving efficiently.

 To learn more, access our SAP Help Portal.

 

R&D:

Project assistant capabilities for the Joule copilot: read cost and revenue details of project objects

Project controllers and R&D teams often spend a lot of time gathering cost and revenue details from multiple reports, especially when they need to compare actuals, planned values, commitments, or fiscal‑year breakdowns. Navigating through different project objects, whether a project, WBS element, network, or network activity, can slow decision‑making and make routine checks feel heavier than they need to be. With Joule acting as a project assistant, users can simply ask for the financial picture of any project object and instantly receive details such as actual cost, planned cost, commitments, or revenue, including breakdowns by fiscal year, cost component, or value category. This conversational access removes the need to open complex reports or consolidate information manually, giving teams a clearer view of project performance in seconds. The result is faster insights, easier financial monitoring, and a more intuitive way for project stakeholders to stay on top of their work.

 Consult our SAP Help Page for more information.

 

Streamlining batch master insights and navigation with Joule

Reviewing batch master data often requires users to navigate across multiple fields and apps to check details such as material, plant, batch status, manufacturing date, shelf-life expiry date, or supplier, which can slow decision-making and increase the risk of missed insights under tight timelines. With Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private edition, users can describe their requirement in natural language and instantly retrieve relevant batch master information, then navigate directly to the Manage Batches object page for deeper analysis, updates, or next actions. This conversational approach streamlines access to batch insights, enhances user experience, and improves operational efficiency through faster, more intuitive interaction.

 

Embedded AI

Finance:

AI-assisted fixed asset key figures explanation

Fixed asset reporting often raises questions that require deep accounting knowledge, especially when values are influenced by midyear acquisitions, depreciation keys, or complex calculation rules. In many organizations, answering these questions means digging through configuration tables or asking specialists to explain how a specific number was derived. With AI‑assisted explanations in Joule, users can simply request a breakdown of any fixed asset key figure and immediately see where the values come from and how they were calculated. This natural‑language guidance helps preparers and reviewers understand depreciation logic and valuation outcomes without manual analysis, reducing the risk of errors or compliance issues. As a result, teams can resolve fixed‑asset questions more quickly and consistently, with far less effort during period close or audits.

 

 

AI-assisted settlement rule proposal for asset capitalization  

Creating settlement rules for investment measures can be a painstaking task, especially when projects or internal orders need to settle to fixed assets and no automatic guidance is available. Finance teams often spend time determining the right receivers and percentages, increasing the risk of inconsistencies across measures or late adjustments when assets are capitalized. With AI‑assisted settlement rule proposals in Joule, users receive an initial suggestion for full settlement to fixed assets and can easily refine the proposal by adjusting percentages or selecting different receivers. This conversational support reduces manual effort and helps ensure more accurate asset class determination for final capitalization. The result is a smoother, more reliable settlement process that supports finance teams without adding complexity.

 

 

Supply Chain:

AI-assisted goods receipt analysis for bordero documents 

Goods receipt processing for bordero documents can be slow and error‑prone, especially when teams need to manually review carrier paperwork, create corresponding freight orders, and verify that all information matches what’s in the system. These manual checks often lead to delays at the gate, longer truck turnaround times, and unnecessary waiting for logistics partners. With SAP Document AI analyzing bordero documents, the system can automatically extract key details, propose a freight order, and highlight discrepancies between the uploaded document and existing freight data. This reduces the time spent on validations, improves accuracy, and helps teams process inbound goods more efficiently. The smoother flow not only enhances operational performance but can also reduce yard congestion by keeping vehicles moving.

 

Summary

As you look across the innovations in this release, a clear pattern emerges: AI is no longer something users “go to”, it’s something that quietly supports them throughout their day. Whether it’s a cash manager starting the morning with a clear liquidity picture, a service agent navigating directly to the right contract, or a warehouse coordinator checking outbound tasks with a simple question, Joule and embedded AI make these moments feel more connected and intuitive. These capabilities reduce the friction of everyday work, giving teams more room to focus on decisions, collaboration, and the tasks that genuinely move the business forward. And while this release delivers meaningful improvements on its own, it also lays the foundation for even more intelligent assistance, with agents and AI features becoming part of the natural rhythm of how people use SAP Cloud ERP. It’s a step toward a future where systems feel less like tools and more like partners, helping organizations work smarter, not harder. 

Thank you for taking the time to explore the latest updates of AI‑driven capabilities in SAP Cloud ERP Private Edition with us. If you’d like to stay informed about upcoming features, deep dives, and enablement content, make sure to follow updates tagged with PSCC_Enablement in the SAP Community. You’re also welcome to connect with me (@Yannick_PTT) both in the community and on LinkedIn for more insights and continuous updates.

For a deeper look into the intelligent capabilities delivered in this release, you can explore the full documentation on the SAP Help Portal.

More information

  • What’s New Viewer
  • From Innovation to Impact: SAP Cloud ERP Private 2025 Release (SAP News Center)
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition Community
  • Help Portal SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • What's New for Joule Capabilities
  • SAP Roadmap Explorer for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • SAP Business Suite Product Expert Training H1 2026

entry.title: Increase business outcomes by unleashing the power of AI agents
entry.content:
Co-Authors: Alessandro Biagi, Trinidad MARTINEZ GEA, Thiago Mendes, MostafaSharaf, Yatsea Li

Recently, we have kick-started a webinar series of Reimagine your customer’s business processes with AI Agents with agenda as below:

TitleSchedule
Session 1 - Increase business outcomes by unleashing the power of AI agentsCompleted. Recording and presentation available here.
Session 2 – From Conversational to Autonomous AI Agents

5-6 May 2026.  Register

Session 3 – Take your AI Agents to the next level 7-9 July 2026. Register

In the first webinar Increase business outcomes by unleashing the power of AI agents, we have showcased a prototype use case featuring a custom AI agent (Escalation Assistant) developed with Joule Studio Agent Builder, which is designed to support a Support Manager in IT Consultancy industry by streamlining the escalation-to-resolution process. In this blog post, we would like to recap the webinar and walk you through its end-to-end implementation journey. 

Let's start with some background introduction about AI Agent in general and SAP context. 

Brief Overview

AI Agent or Agentic AI

Nowadays,  AI Agent or Agentic AI is really a hit. You may have heard of Agentic Workflow, Agentic Automation, Agentic AI or AI Agent etc. in enterprise context. In the software development, Agentic Coding or Coding Agent may help you to do much of coding on your behalf with your directing.

So, what does agentic really mean? Let us understand its fundamental concept with some examples.

  • Agentic workflows are AI-driven processes that use autonomous agents to complete complex tasks with minimal human oversight. For example, an AI agent (customer support assistant) receives a customer complaint, analyzes sentiment, retrieves order data from ERP,  research and proposes a resolution, drafts a response email, and logs the case—end to end.
  • Agentic automation is the use of AI agents to perform tasks autonomously, going beyond traditional automation's rule-based execution. One example in procurement-to-pay, a procurement agent monitors supply chain risks, checking procurement policy, compares vendors, negotiates via email, and creates purchase orders based on real-time conditions.
  • Agentic AI refers to autonomous artificial intelligence systems that can act independently to achieve goals, using planning, reasoning, and learning to execute tasks without constant human oversight. Think of a financial planning agent that analyses cash flow trends, runs forecasting models, simulates scenarios, and recommends budget adjustments.

Okay, how are these Agentic systems or AI Agents different from the traditional workflow, robotics process automation(RPA), traditional AI?

Traditional workflows and RPA follow fixed rules. Traditional AI predicts or classifies.

Agentic systems or AI Agents, by contrast, exhibit a higher level of autonomy by leveraging state-of-art LLM's capabilities in planning, reasoning, reflection, and tool usage (e.g., APIs, databases) as illustrated in the diagram below.

In short, traditional automation executes tasks. Agentic AI pursues goals. And the shift from task execution to goal-driven autonomy unlocks new business outcomes.

 

Joule Skill and AI Agent

Let's zoom into SAP context about building custom AI Agent. There are three approaches featured as below. 

Specifically, we'll focus on Joule Studio in this blog post, which enables you to build custom Joule Skills and AI Agents through low-code/no-code approach.

So, what are the differences between Joule Skills and AI Agents?

In short, Skills follow the notion of “Tell me exactly what to do, and I’ll execute it",  while AI Agents operate on the principle of “Tell me the problem, and I’ll determine the best way to solve it.” 

Joule Skills are rule-based and deterministic. You define the workflow, the sequence of steps, validations, and system calls in advance. The problem and the solution path are both clearly specified. Skills can orchestrate and integrate with APIs, processes, and automations etc. with nature language understanding capability.

They are ideal for structured, repeatable tasks that require reliability and control.

Examples of Joule Skills:

  • A skill for Invoice creation in SAP HANA Cloud: Creating an invoice in SAP S/4HANA Cloud based on details provided by the user in natural language and performing some predefined checks.
  • A skill for Vendor Selection in procurement involving multiple system: Fetching detailed vendor data, purchase request, RFQ information, and supply chain risk data from multiple systems e.g. SAP Ariba and 3rd party system through API, then feeding into the vendor screening process defined as a decision table in SAP Build Process Automation in combination of price, delivery , and risk level etc for vendor selection. 
  • ...

On the other hand, AI Agents handle complex or ambiguous tasks where the solution path is not predefined or just too many solution paths to be explicitly defined. They break problems into manageable subtasks and decide dynamically what to do next using advanced planning and reasoning, mimicking human problem-solving with the help of LLMs.

Agents can autonomously use tools like Joule Skill, Document Grounding, Calculator, and Model Context Protocol(MCP) Servers or Sub-agents for collaboration. 

Examples of AI Agents:

  • A procurement advisor agent:  Autonomously retrieving purchase request and vendor data,  assessing supply chain risk with public data, checking procurement policy and vendor contract,  recommending vendor and negotiating with vendor via email with human supervision etc.

  • A customer escalation assistant: The agent retrieves the support incident associated to an escalation request, checks escalation policy,  proposes the possible solutions by searching web and internal knowledge base, and drafts customer communication.

  • ...

Next, let's move on to a practical use case of custom AI Agent(escalation assistant) with Joule Studio for Escalation Management in customer service, and see how the escalation assistant help to streamline the process of escalation-to-resolution with Agentic AI.

 

Use Case - Escalation Assistant

Emma, a support manager at an IT consultancy company, manages customer escalations to ensure customer satisfaction and continuous process improvement. Therefore, the essential need is to expedite escalation to resolution and provide responsive customer communication.

The Traditional Workflow of  Escalation Management

Let's examine Emma's usual routine tasks of handling an escalation request without any help of Escalation Assistant. 

  1. Jane from the customer, has been frustratedly waiting for a resolution to their support incident, which has been reported 30 days ago, now have significant impact on their daily business. She sends an escalation request about to Emma through e-mail.
  2. Upon receiving the escalation request, Emma first needs to check and qualify the associated incident to the escalation request against the company’s escalation policy.
  3. If qualified, Emma drafts and sends a quick response email to reassure the customer. Otherwise, reply the customer about the rejection of the escalation with clear explanation. 
  4. As part of the escalation policy, if the incident priority is low or medium, she can increase it to high and notify the responsible support consultant and the customer about the escalation.
  5. Emma attempts to find a solution by searching the web and internal knowledge base about the relevant topic and respond the customer with the solution via an email.
  6. ... 

In a traditional escalation management workflow, Emma has to manually manage multiple touchpoints including customer interactions, escalation requests from various channels, the ticketing system, company escalation policy and support consultants etc. This process is trivia and time-consuming. Next, let's see how we can help Emma to automate and streamline the process of escalation to resolution with AI Agent.

Agentic Automation of Escalation Management

Instead of manually performing these tasks, Emma asks help from the Escalation Assistant (a custom AI Agent built with Joule Studio Agent Builder), which automates  the end-to-end process on her behalf.

Here list the high-level the capabilities of AI Agent in Joule Studio mapping with Emma's manual tasks in the workflow of escalation management.

  1.  Escalation policy is checked against the ticket using document grounding.
  2. Web search of issue topic through MCP(Model Context Protocol) servers.
  3. Knowledge base search about the similar issue are handled through Joule skills and actions.
  4. Retrieving and updating Incident ticket are handled through Joule skills and actions.
  5. The email communication about status and  the solution is sent by a process automation within a Joule skill.

Final End-to-End Demo

Let's have a look at the end-to-end demo to see how Escalation Assistant can help Emma to streamline and automate the escalation-to-resolution process.

 

Design of Escalation Assistant

Now that we have an idea about the use case. Let's move to the design of the agent. We'll convert the process into an agentic diagram, which illustrate the design of the multiple-agent systems and how each agent collaborate together towards the end goal.

Agentic Diagram 

The design of Escalation Assistant follow the principle of modular multiple agent system. Each AI Agent is specialised in a specific task. The root agent Escalation Assistant act as an orchestrator, responsible for orchestrating the other agents in collaborating together towards the end goals.
Here list the agents of Escalation Assistant. Click each item to find out its configurations in Joule Studio, such as expertise, instructions etc. which can be replicated in your own Joule Studio.

AI AgentResponsibilityRoleUsed by
Escalation AssistantOrchestrating the other three sub agentsRoot AgentEntry Point
Escalation AdvisorChecking company policySub AgentEscalation Assistant
Advisory ExpertProviding a straightforward solution by leveraging another two sub agents: the Web Searcher and Knowledge Base SearcherSub AgentEscalation Assistant
Web SearcherSearching the web with the generated querySub AgentAdvisory Expert
Knowledge Base SearcherSearching the knowledge base with the generated querySub AgentAdvisory Expert
Service Call HandlerRetrieving and updating service callsRoot AgentEscalation Assistant

 

Solution Architecture

If we look at the solution architecture of the whole solution, we can see how  our Joule Skills integrate seamlessly with various systems via destinations.

We use SAP Field Service Management for handling Service Calls in the PoC, that said, it could be SAP Service Cloud or SAP S/4HANA Cloud solution etc. with ticketing capability.

It connects our Smart Advisory Companion back-end application for searching internal knowledge base, which is built in Python and deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry runtime. The back-end leverages several powerful SAP BTP capabilities, including: Generative AI Hub for accessing and utilising Large Language Models properly grounded with the appropriate business context.

The Joule Skills will serve as tools for the Joule Agents. Both are implemented using Joule Studio in SAP Build and deployed directly into the Joule tenant within SAP BTP.

The agents will also rely on some MCP Servers to accomplish their job, such as web search.

With each user interaction through the Joule Application Client, Joule intelligently selects the most relevant agent to execute, based on their descriptions, ensuring an efficient and contextual response every time.

 

End-to-End Implementation Journey

By now we have gone through the use case and technical design, let's dive into the end-to-end implementation journey of Escalation Assistant with Joule Studio Agent Builder.

1. Reusable Joule Skills

Before going into the implementation details of the AI agents from the agentic diagram, let’s just have a quick overview on some Joule Skills reused in multiple agents.

  • Execute Request: A generic Joule Skill as API Gateway to execute any OData or RESTful API, which is used by Service Call Handler for reading and writing Service Calls.
  • Send Email: A reusable Joule Skill to send email with dynamic input parameters such as email title, recipient, message body etc.

2. Service Call Handler

Let's start with the implementation of Service Call Handler agent which is responsible for reading and writing service calls in SAP Field Service Management.

Demo Recording:

 

3.Escalation Advisor

The retrieved service call information by Service Call Handler Agent is handed over to another agent Escalation Advisor for checking the service call against the company's escalation policy to see if its associated escalation request is qualified as an escalation. Let’s see its configuration. 

 

4. Advisory Expert

Once the escalation request is qualified as an escalation, then the Advisory Expert agent will work on some possible solution proposal to the escalated issue by searching the web and internal knowledge base about the related topic about the escalation though another two sub-agents Web Searcher and Knowledge Base Searcher, then crafting a solution based on the search results. Let's check out  its implementation.

 

5. Escalation Assistant

Finally, the Escalation Assistant orchestrates and chains the sub agents to collaborate together throughout the end-to-end process of escalation handling. Let's examine its configuration details.

 

6. Testing

Once the agents are configured, let's test the Escalation Assistant to examine its execution detail with built-in testing tool of Joule Studio. It may take multiple iterations of tuning and testing of agent configurations until it yields expected outcome.

We have just completed the end-to-end implementation journey of Escalation Assistant. Let's review some best practices.

Best Practices of Creating Joule Agents

  • Be clear and specific: Define a clear goal and the steps to reach it.

  • Use step-by-step logic: Break complex rules into simple, ordered lists.

  • Provide actionable instructions: Ensure every step leads to a clear action (usually a tool call).

  • Avoid conflicting rules: Establish a clear priority for rules to prevent confusion.

  • Restrict the toolset: Grant access only to the tools essential for the agent's job.

  • Use English for all design: Write all core instructions and tool descriptions in English for consistency.

  • Follow the design principle of modularity and reusability: Break down the complex use case into multiple modular agents. Each sub-agent is specialised in a single role with clear and specific instructions. Orchestrate the multiple sub agents to collaborate towards the goal.  

  • Test throughout with different configuration: Test with different LLMs and agent configurations to find out the best-fit. 

More details about the best practices available here.

 

Additional Resources 

In addition, here you can learn more resoures how to design a Joule agents in Joule Studio.

  • Learning Journey: Introducing Joule Studio in SAP Build
  • Developer Tutorial: Use Joule Studio to Create an Agent
  • Discovery Center: Build custom AI Agents with Joule Studio
  • Check out our Github Repository of Escalation Assistant
  • Review our Webinar Recoding: Increase business outcomes by unleashing the power of AI agents

 

Wrap up

In this blog post, we have learned the overview of AI Agent, Joule Skill and Joule Agent. Joule skill is ideal for structured, repeatable tasks that require reliability and control. And Joule Agent handle complex or ambiguous tasks where the solution path is not predefined or too many solution paths to be explicitly described. In addition, we have also gone through the end-to-end journey of implementing an Joule Agent about Escalation Assistant.  If you would like to learn more about custom AI Agent,  please register to our webinars about Reimagine your customer’s business processes with AI Agents.

entry.title: From Sales Data to Design Blueprints: How RPT-1 Enabled Our Fashion Inverse Design Engine
entry.content:
What We Built

The Fashion Trend Alchemist is a fully functional inverse design engine that turns historical sales data into concrete design recommendations — complete with product visualizations, a catchy product name, and compelling sales copy. It works across product categories: coats, t-shirts, dresses, trousers, even shoes and sunglasses. The system adapts to any product type without retraining or reconfiguration.
 
How it creates a product — the full pipeline:
The product creation workflow: from context selection through AI enrichment and RPT-1 prediction to the final product profile with images, name, and sales text. The refine loop lets users iterate on any design decision.
As shown in the diagram above, product creation flows through four core phases:
1. Context Setup — Select a product category (e.g., men's winter coats), define filters and date ranges, and let the system build a curated context of top and bottom performers from the sales data
2. Enrichment — A Vision LLM analyzes product images to extract detailed design attributes (collar type, button style, fabric weight...) that don't exist in the raw database
3. Prediction — The user specifies which attributes to lock and which to predict; RPT-1 fills in the optimal values using in-context learning from the enriched dataset
4. Product Profile — The system generates multi-view product images, proposes a product name, and drafts sales copy — creating a complete, tangible design concept that can be refined and saved to a collection
 
Technical Architecture
This isn't a Jupyter notebook experiment — it's a deployed, interactive application where users explore design decisions in real time.
 
The Fashion Trend Alchemist runs on SAP BTP's Kyma runtime as a three-container deployment. The React frontend communicates with a Fastify/Node.js backend that orchestrates three AI services: RPT-1 for attribute prediction and GPT-4.1 for data enrichment and text generation (both via SAP AI Core), and a **self-hosted Z-Image Turbo** instance (product visualization, deployed as a Kyma microservice). PostgreSQL, Redis, and SeaweedFS handle data persistence, caching, and image storage respectively.
 
How It Works: Designing a Men's Winter Coat
Let's walk through a concrete example to see each phase in action: a product manager wants to know what the next successful men's winter coat should look like.
 
Building the Context
The user selects "Coats" as the product type, filters for winter seasons and men's wear, and the system queries a large transaction dataset for matching products.
For each matching product, the system calculates a velocity score — first computing units sold per day of availability (transaction count divided by days between first and last sale), then converting this to a percentile rank (0-100) across all matching products. A coat that sold 200 units in 30 days scores higher than one that sold 200 units over 6 months. The system then selects the top and bottom performers to create a balanced context showing RPT-1 clear examples of "success" and "failure."
 
Enriching Products with Design Attributes
Fashion databases store generic columns: color, material, product group. But a coat's success depends on collar type, insulation level, and closure style — attributes that don't exist in the raw data.

We solve this with AI enrichment. First, an LLM generates a product-specific attribute schema (e.g., `collar_type`, `button_style`, `insulation_level` with their possible values). Then, GPT-4.1 analyzes each product's image and extracts these attributes as structured data.
Real-time enrichment with progress tracking — GPT-4.1 extracts structured design attributes from product images.
 
RPT-1 in Action: The Prediction
Now the core moment. The enriched context — ~50 products with their detailed attributes and velocity scores — is sent to RPT-1. The user constructs a query row where some attributes are fixed and others contain `[PREDICT]` placeholders.
 
 
The interface uses a three-column layout:

- Locked (left): Attributes the user wants to fix — "I know I want a Navy coat"
- AI-Predicted (center): Attributes RPT-1 should determine — "What collar type? What button style?"
- Excluded (right): Attributes irrelevant to this prediction
 
The user sets a target success score and clicks "Transmute." RPT-1 receives the full context plus the query row in a single API call — no model training, no feature engineering, no pipeline orchestration. Formatted tabular data in, predictions out.

The entire ML layer is essentially: _format data as rows → add query row with `[PREDICT]` → call API → parse response_. The system complexity lives in data preparation, not model management.
 
Bringing It to Life: The Full Product Profile
The predicted attributes are combined with locked values to generate a complete product profile. The system translates the attribute combination into structured image prompts and produces multiple product views via a self-hosted image generation model running on Kyma — using category-aware prompt templates (coats use ghost mannequin photography; footwear uses product pair shots; accessories get contextual framing). This is completed by a catchy product name and a sales text.
 
The result isn't an abstract list of attributes — it's a concrete product concept that a designer or product manager can evaluate, iterate on, and refine. The refine loop shown in the pipeline diagram lets users adjust any attribute and regenerate until they're satisfied, then save the design to a collection.
 
What We Learned About RPT-1
Prediction Becomes a Data Formatting Problem
RPT-1 is pretrained and uses in-context learning — you don't train the model, you provide examples and a query. The API structure is remarkably simple:
The entire prediction integration is ~50 lines of TypeScript. No training scripts, no model serialization, no serving infrastructure. This drastically lowers the barrier for teams outside ML/data science — if you can format tabular data, you can use RPT-1.

Dynamic schemas come free: coats need `collar_type` and `button_style`, sunglasses need `lens_tint` and `frame_shape`. With traditional ML, changing features means re-encoding, retraining, revalidating. With RPT-1, just change the columns.
 
Validating RPT-1 in the Fashion Domain
Our goal wasn't to build a production prediction system — it was to test whether RPT-1's in-context learning could handle fashion data meaningfully. To validate this, we inverted the workflow: instead of predicting attributes from success scores (inverse design), we tested how well RPT-1 predicts success from attributes (forward prediction). This serves as a sanity check — if the model understands attribute-success relationships, it can leverage them for inverse predictions.

We benchmarked RPT-1 against traditional gradient boosting models (XGBoost, CatBoost) across five product categories using an 80/20 random split:
Model
R² 
NDCG 
Hit@25
RPT-1
+0.302
0.954
60.5%
XGBoost
+0.267
0.946
55.1%
CatBoost
+0.258
0.945
 56.4%
Aggregated across five product categories. RPT-1 matches established gradient boosting models on absolute fit (R²) and edges them out on ranking quality (NDCG, Hit@25) — the metrics that matter most for separating winners from losers.
 
The scatter plot below shows this concretely for ladies' dresses: top performers (Q4, green) cluster where predicted, while bottom performers (Q1, red) separate clearly. The model isn't perfect, but the quartile separation confirms RPT-1 successfully learned from the in-context examples.
Actual vs. predicted rank for Ladies All-Season Dresses (ρ = 0.626). Clear quartile separation demonstrates RPT-1 effectively distinguishes high from low performers using only in-context examples.
 
The key finding: RPT-1 worked out-of-the-box for fashion data without domain-specific tuning. For teams exploring tabular foundation models in new domains, this suggests the pretrained capabilities transfer broadly. RPT-1's in-context learning architecture particularly shines in low-data scenarios — some niche categories had only ~23 items in the context, where RPT-1 handled these gracefully.
 
 
 
 
Context Quality Matters More Than Context Structure
We systematically tested what affects RPT-1's prediction quality:
 
Row and column ordering — by design, RPT-1's architecture is invariant to the ordering of rows and columns, so reordering should not affect predictions (up to minor numerical artifacts). Our experiments confirmed this: shuffling context rows or reordering columns produced no measurable difference in results — exactly as expected.

Column naming — inherently relevant, though RPT-1 proved surprisingly resilient. Even when we degraded names to synonyms, German translations, or numeric codes, performance variations were minor and inconsistent. Descriptive column names remain a best practice, but the model handles imperfect naming gracefully.

 
Spearman correlation across naming conditions (Original, Synonyms, German, Mixed, Legacy, Chaos, Numeric). RPT-1 proved robust across conditions in our dataset, though meaningful column names remain a best practice for optimal predictions.
 
Out-of-distribution contamination — high impact. When wrong products polluted the context (e.g., shirts mixed into a hoodies dataset), prediction quality degraded significantly.
 
Spearman correlation drops as OOD contamination increases from 0% to 40%. Clean data matters far more than clever formatting.

The takeaway: RPT-1 handles structural variations gracefully — row/column ordering has no effect, and naming degradation had limited impact in our tests. Your primary effort should go into data cleaning and proper normalization.

Challenges We Faced and How We Solved Them
Real-World Data is Messy

Problem: Product catalogs contain labeling errors — shirts categorized as hoodies, skirts labeled as pants. In-context learning amplifies these errors: garbage in, garbage out.

Solution: Our Vision LLM enrichment returns a `mismatchConfidence` score (0-100) alongside extracted attributes. Products scoring above 80 are flagged for human review. The user sees a prefiltered list and can correct or exclude problematic items before prediction. What started as an afterthought became essential.

GenAI Output is Unpredictable

Problem: LLM outputs can be wrong or malformed — invalid JSON, attributes outside valid ranges, unexpected structures.

Solution: Multi-layer guardrails:

- Prompting with positive examples and explicit output structure
- Runtime validation with Zod schemas to catch structural errors
- Attribute values validated against the ontology's allowed options
- Retry logic with exponential backoff (up to 3 attempts)
 
Users can manually adjust results or send them back to the LLM with feedback.
 
Consistency Across Generated Images
Problem: Three product views (front, back, model) must show the same design. Different prompts lead to inconsistent products.

Solution: The LLM generates a single shared `productDescription` used as the consistent core across all views, combined with view-specific prefixes and category-aware templates (wearables use ghost mannequin photography; footwear uses product pair shots).
 
Closing

Three takeaways for your next project:

1. RPT-1 turns prediction into a data selection & formatting problem. No training pipeline, no ML expertise required — just clean tabular data with a success metric.

2. Context quality is paramount. Row and column ordering has no effect on RPT-1 by design, and the model proved resilient to naming variations in our experiments — though descriptive column names remain recommended. What matters most is clean, relevant data: wrong items in the context degrade predictions significantly.

3. Build guardrails around GenAI. Validate LLM outputs with schemas, constrain responses to allowed values, and keep humans in the loop for edge cases.
 
 
Developed at BTP Solution Advisory APAC by Danylo Polishchuk and Nils Dittrich.
 
For questions or collaboration opportunities, connect with us on LinkedIn: Danylo Polishchuk | Nils Dittrich.
 
Want to see the Fashion Trend Alchemist in action? Check out the demo video for a walkthrough of the complete workflow.

Want to try RPT-1 yourself? Explore the RPT-1 Playground to test tabular predictions with your own data.

entry.title: How AI and Machine Learning Are Quietly Transforming SAP Integrations — My Perspective as a Beginner
entry.content:
The Gap I Noticed in Traditional Integration
In a typical iFlow I built recently, I had to manually define rules like:
∙ If amount is greater than 100000 — route to high value channel
∙ If region is not IN or US — mark as invalid
∙ If quantity is zero — reject the item
These rules work fine when business logic is simple and stable. But what happens when the rules keep changing? What if the threshold for “high value” changes every quarter? What if new regions get added every month? A human has to go in, update the Groovy script, redeploy, test again. That is time consuming and error prone.
This is exactly where AI and ML start making sense in SAP integrations.

 

Where AI/ML Actually Fits in SAP

1. Intelligent Data Mapping

“Mail Based Batch Processing iFlow — routing logic manually defined for each invoice type” 

One of the most frustrating parts of SAP CPI for a beginner is Message Mapping. You have to manually connect every source field to every target field. For simple scenarios this is manageable. But imagine mapping two enterprise systems with hundreds of fields each.
SAP has been working on AI assisted mapping where the system suggests field mappings based on field names, data types and historical mapping patterns. Instead of manually dragging every line, the AI proposes the mapping and you just review and approve. This saves enormous time in large integration projects.

This is my data in Postman


2. Anomaly Detection in Integration Flows

“Manually setting threshold values in Content Modifier — AI could dynamically adjust these thresholds based on patterns”

Imagine your iFlow processes 10000 invoices every day. Suddenly one day 200 invoices fail. Is that normal? Is something broken? Did a source system change its format?
ML models trained on historical message processing logs can detect these anomalies automatically. Instead of waiting for someone to notice failures in the monitor, the system alerts you proactively. SAP Integration Advisor and SAP Alert Notification Service are already moving in this direction.


3. Predictive Error Handling

“Manually defined routing conditions in SAP CPI Router — AI could learn and suggest these rules automatically based on historical data”

Traditional error handling in CPI is reactive — something fails, you get an alert, you investigate. ML changes this to proactive. By analyzing patterns in past failures — which steps fail most often, at what times, with what kind of payloads — a model can predict which messages are likely to fail before they actually do and reroute them automatically.


4. Natural Language to iFlow

“Custom business logic written manually in Groovy — imagine describing this in plain English and having AI generate it automatically” 

This one genuinely surprised me when I read about it. Researchers and SAP teams are experimenting with systems where you describe what you want in plain English — something like “receive invoices from vendor system, validate amounts, send high value ones to finance team via email” — and the system automatically generates a working iFlow.
We are not fully there yet but with large language models becoming more capable this is not science fiction anymore.


5. AI Powered Document Understanding
In my Scenario 3 assignment I processed invoices that were already structured XML. But in the real world invoices come as PDFs, scanned images, emails and all kinds of unstructured formats. SAP Document Information Extraction — part of SAP AI Business Services — uses ML to read these unstructured documents and extract fields like invoice number, vendor name, amount and date automatically. This extracted data can then flow directly into an SAP CPI iFlow for further processing.

 

What SAP Is Actually Building
SAP has been investing heavily in an umbrella platform called SAP AI Core and SAP AI Launchpad. These are the infrastructure layers that allow AI models to be trained, deployed and consumed across SAP systems.
On top of that, specific AI services have been released:
∙ SAP Document Information Extraction — extracts data from unstructured documents
∙ SAP Business Entity Recognition — identifies business entities in text
∙ SAP Translation Hub — ML powered translation for global deployments
∙ SAP Intelligent Robotic Process Automation — combines RPA with ML for smarter automation
All of these can be connected to SAP Integration Suite, meaning your iFlows can call these AI services as external APIs and enrich your integration logic with intelligence.

A Small Real World Example
Let me paint a picture. Suppose a company receives invoices from 50 different vendors. Each vendor sends invoices in a slightly different format — different field names, different structures, different currencies.
Without AI:
∙ A developer manually creates one mapping per vendor
∙ 50 vendors means 50 mappings to maintain
∙ Every time a vendor changes their format someone has to update the mapping
With AI:
∙ ML model learns the pattern of each vendor’s format from historical data
∙ Automatically adapts mappings when formats change slightly
∙ Flags only major structural changes for human review
∙ Developer effort reduces from weeks to hours

 

My Honest Take


I am still a beginner with SAP CPI. I am still figuring out XPath expressions and Groovy scripts. But even at this level I can see that the manual rule based approach has limits. The more I work with integration flows the more I appreciate why AI is being brought into this space.
The integrations of tomorrow will not just move data — they will understand it, validate it intelligently, predict problems before they happen and adapt to changes automatically. SAP is clearly building towards that future and I think it is genuinely exciting for anyone working in this space today.

References and Further Reading
∙ SAP AI Business Services on SAP Discovery Center
∙ SAP Integration Advisor Documentation
∙ SAP AI Core on SAP Help Portal
∙ SAP Community blogs on Intelligent Integration

 This blog reflects my personal observations and learning while working with SAP Integration Suite as part of my coursework. All scenarios mentioned are based on hands-on assignment work.

entry.title: Building Enterprise-Grade RAG: Seven Architectural Innovations Beyond Vector Search
entry.content:
Building Enterprise-Grade RAG: Seven Architectural Innovations Beyond Vector Search

A concise technical guide for production RAG systems

Executive Summary

Traditional Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems rely primarily on semantic similarity through vector embeddings, but this approach fails in enterprise environments with large, diverse document collections. This article presents seven architectural innovations that address fundamental RAG limitations, delivering 60-75% faster queries, 85-95% precision, and 60-75% token reduction compared to traditional approaches.

The Five Problems with Traditional RAG

Problem 1: Semantic-Only Retrieval Cannot Distinguish Context

Traditional RAG encodes queries and searches all embeddings by cosine similarity, assuming semantic closeness equals relevance. This breaks down when users need contextual filtering: "show me vendor A contracts from 2024" retrieves any semantically similar contract language regardless of vendor identity, document type, or fiscal year. Vector embeddings capture meaning but not categorical properties—two vendor contracts from different years produce nearly identical embeddings because they use identical legal language and pricing terminology. Result: 30-50% false positives from wrong vendors, wrong years, or wrong document types. Users must manually verify every result, defeating automation's purpose.

Problem 2: Structural Blindness Destroys Document Organization

Character-count chunking (typically 1000 characters with 200 overlap) ignores document structure, splitting at arbitrary boundaries regardless of semantic sections. A document with clear sections-Introduction, Technical Specifications, Pricing, Compliance gets carved into chunk 1, chunk 2, chunk N without preserving these boundaries. When users query "show me technical specifications," the system retrieves random paragraphs mentioning "technical" scattered across the document rather than the complete specifications section. Even perfect vector search cannot overcome structural destruction during chunking, related content sits in disconnected chunks requiring manual reconstruction.

Problem 3: Table Destruction Loses Critical Structured Data

Tables contain 40-60% of critical enterprise information-pricing matrices, technical specifications, comparison charts, timelines, responsibility assignments. Text-based chunking catastrophically fails: a 20-row pricing table gets split across chunks, each missing column headers or context. Converting tables to linear text loses two-dimensional relationships. Worse, tables are typically introduced by explanatory paragraphs ("The following pricing applies to annual contracts only...") that get separated during chunking, leaving the LLM to interpret data without crucial qualifiers. Result: 60-80% of tables rendered unusable or misinterpreted.

Problem 4: Chat History Token Explosion from AI Response Storage

Most RAG systems store complete conversation history-user messages plus AI responses for follow-up context. A typical 5-turn conversation: user message 1 (50 tokens) + AI response 1 (2000 tokens) + user message 2 (30 tokens) + AI response 2 (1800 tokens) + user message 3 (40 tokens) + AI response 3 (2200 tokens) = 6,120 total tokens stored. Actual new information: 120 tokens from user messages. AI responses are synthesized from documents already in the database—storing them is pure redundancy adding no new context. As conversations extend to 10-15 turns, history consumes 20,000-30,000 tokens, leaving less context window for retrieved documents and increasing API costs exponentially.

Problem 5: One-Size-Fits-All Responses Waste Resources

Traditional RAG treats all queries identically: embed → vector search → retrieve → generate LLM response. But 30-40% of enterprise queries want document inventory (lists, counts, metadata): "list all contracts," "how many technical documents?" These need simple database lookups returning structured lists , not vector searches followed by verbose LLM generation. The remaining 60-70% need content extraction: "summarize technical requirements," "compare pricing across vendors." These require full RAG pipeline. Traditional systems waste compute and time generating responses for inventory queries that should be instant metadata lookups, degrading user experience with slow, verbose responses when users want clean, fast lists.

The Seven Innovations

Innovation 1: LLM-Powered Document Classification

Use an LLM to classify each chunk into semantic section types (Introduction, Technical Specifications, Requirements, Pricing, Timelines, Compliance, Risks) based on content analysis rather than arbitrary character boundaries. After standard text splitting creates chunks, pass each through a zero-temperature LLM with constrained classification prompt returning a single category label stored as chunk metadata. This adds some time consumed per chunk during ingestion but enables powerful section-aware retrieval and saves time during retrieval, users can query "show me pricing sections" and retrieve complete, focused pricing content rather than scattered paragraphs. Classification uses temperature=0 for deterministic results and minimal token output to reduce latency. Impact: Section-specific queries improve from 50-70% precision to 85-95%, with 60-80% reduction in irrelevant chunks.

Innovation 2: Dual-Stream Chunking (Text + Tables)

Implement parallel extraction pipelines-one for flowing text, one for structured data. During document processing, detect and extract tables, charts, and matrices as complete units separately from text extraction, preserving internal structure entirely. Convert tables to Markdown format for LLM readability, capture surrounding context (preceding 2-3 paragraphs explaining scope and assumptio, and infer semantic category from position. Store tables as independent chunks with specialized metadata: structure type, dimensions, contextual flags. Enrich table content with context prefix: "Context: The following pricing applies to annual contracts only. [Table: Pricing Matrix] | Plan | Cost | ..." This embeds business meaning with data structure, dramatically improving semantic search. Impact: 100% table structure preservation (vs 20-30% with text splitting), 90%+ precision for structured data queries, 50-70% better semantic relevance from context enrichment.

Innovation 3: Rich Metadata Layer

Attach comprehensive hierarchical metadata at three levels: folder/organizational (inherited from directory structure), document (file properties and custom business tags from document management systems), and chunk (section classification). Metadata captures identity (name, type, author), organizational context (department, region, category), temporal attributes (creation date, fiscal period), and custom business tags (vendor, client, project, status). Use inheritance pattern: folder-level tags apply to all contained documents, document-level tags override folder tags, chunk-level tags add section granularity. Store metadata as structured JSON alongside embeddings, enabling efficient database queries. On initialization, scan corpus to discover available metadata values across dimensions, building a catalog of unique vendors, document types, regions, years. Impact: Enables precise contextual queries combining 3-5 filters, reducing search space by 95-98% before vector operations.

Innovation 4: Metadata Pre-Filtering Before Vector Search (The Game Changer)

Execute metadata filtering BEFORE vector similarity search, reducing search space by to only contextually relevant candidates. Five-stage process:
(1) Discover available metadata by scanning corpus—vendors, regions, document types, fiscal years,
(2) LLM extracts relevant filters from user query given the discovered catalog, returning structured constraints like {vendor:[A,C], doc_type:[contract], year:[2024]}.
(3) Build database query matching extracted metadata conditions.
(4) Execute filter reducing 50,000 chunks to 500-1000 pre-qualified chunks (98%+ reduction).
(5) Compute vector similarity only on filtered subset. Modern vector databases (like HANA Cloud) support hybrid queries combining metadata filters with vector similarity. 
Impact: 3x faster, precision jumps from 15% to 95%+ because all results are contextually correct, 60-75% token reduction from tighter context.

Innovation 5: Smart Chat History (User Messages Only)

Store complete conversations in database for audit, but retrieve only user messages from time window (e.g., last 12 hours) when building context for new queries. AI responses are synthesized from documents already in database they add no new information, only consuming tokens. Use context retrieval of user messages only filtered by time window. Pass user-only history to LLM for query consolidation: "Given prior user queries [list], interpret new query in context." Time windowing captures typical work session duration while preventing stale context interference and naturally limiting context growth. Impact: 90-97% token reduction on chat history, faster query processing, better context quality focusing only on user intent refinement trail.

Innovation 6: Intent-Based Query Routing

Classify user intent before processing, routing to appropriate pipeline: lightweight metadata retrieval for inventory queries, full RAG for content extraction. Use fast keyword matching for unambiguous queries ("list documents," "how many," "find all" → LIST intent; "what are," "summarize," "compare," "extract" → ANSWERING intent). LIST intent executes database query returning document names/counts/categories as structured HTML list (no LLM). ANSWERING intent executes full enhanced RAG with metadata filter, vector search, and LLM generation. For ambiguous queries, use lightweight LLM classification or default to ANSWERING. Impact: 10x faster responses for inventory queries, 40-60% lower API costs, better UX matching response type to user expectation (clean lists for inventory, comprehensive synthesis for extraction).

Innovation 7: Production-Grade Resilience

Implement fault-tolerant batch processing for enterprise-scale ingestion (1000+ documents, 100,000+ chunks). Four-layer defense: (1) Batch processing with rate limiting-split into small batches (10-20 chunks per call) with delays between batches (1-2s) and documents (3-5s), (2) Independent document processing-each document is autonomous unit, failure doesn't abort batch, successful documents commit immediately, (3) Progress tracking—real-time logging, error categorization, partial success reporting ("150 succeeded, 8 failed"). (4)Before processing, check database for existing documents by source URL (prevents deduplications and saves time as everytime it runs it only uploads delta documents). 
Impact: Reliable operation at scale, survives transient API failures without intervention, clear progress visibility, partial success delivery (

Performance Impact: Traditional vs Enhanced

Metric

Traditional RAG

Enhanced RAG

Improvement

Query Latency

2-4 seconds

0.5-1.2 seconds

60-75% faster

Chunks Searched

10,000-100,000+ (all)

500-2,000 (1-5% of corpus)

95-98% reduction

Precision

50-70% relevant

85-95% relevant

30-40pp increase

False Positives

30-50% of results

5-15% of results

70-85% reduction

Token Usage (input)

12,000-20,000 tokens

3,000-8,000 tokens

60-75% reduction

Chat History Overhead

6,000-8,000 tokens

150-300 tokens

95-97% reduction

Inventory Query Time

2-4 seconds (full RAG)

0.1-0.3 seconds (metadata)

90-95% faster

Table Retrieval Success

20-40% intact

95-100% intact

3-4x improvement

Ingestion Success Rate

60-80% (fails on errors)

90-98% (fault tolerance)

15-30pp increase

The Core Architectural Insight:
 Metadata filtering before vector search beats pure vector search. By filtering 95-98% of irrelevant chunks using structured metadata before expensive semantic similarity calculations, we achieve simultaneous improvements in speed, accuracy, and efficiency—the rare engineering win-win-win.


Traditional RAG

The New Pipeline

Conclusion: 
These seven innovations transform RAG from prototype demo to production-grade system users can trust. The patterns are platform-agnostic and applicable to any enterprise RAG system over large, diverse document collections with organizational hierarchies, temporal dimensions, and categorical properties.

entry.title: Artificial Intelligence in Organizational Change Management: From Theory to Practice
entry.content:
As a quick recap: in our previous post, we explored the impact of AI across our six

Organizational Change Management (OCM) dimensions:  In our previous post (read the article here), we explored HOW AI impacts the six OCM dimensions.

Now let's get practical: WHAT can you do with it - now?

Based on conversations with change professionals across industries, we've identified three high-impact opportunities where AI is already delivering results in SAP transformations.

Being in mutual exchange with other change professionals, we have identified many opportunities for the improvement of transformation & change projects by using AI capabilities to solve several challenges.

From streamlining content creation to enabling impactful communication on tight budgets and acting as a strategic sparring partner, AI opens new possibilities for tackling challenges and driving innovation in OCM. Below, you’ll find a graphic that presents the three opportunities at a glance.

 

 

 

 

 

1st Opportunity – Transforming Content Creation with AI

Scenario
Your teams produce great ideas—but turning workshop outcomes into on‑brand, stakeholder‑ready assets is slow, manual, inconsistent, and scattered across channels. Deadlines stack up, style guides get bent, and version control becomes a guessing game. You need a way to turn raw inputs into professional, audience‑specific materials without the heavy lifting.

AI Solution
Use generative AI to turn workshop outputs, briefs, and project artifacts into on‑brand deliverables: vision statements, key messages, stakeholder briefings, enablement outlines, and even first‑cut engagement plans. Configure brand and tone guardrails, map stakeholder preferences, and let AI adapt content by audience, channel, and language —reducing formatting work while improving consistency.

Real Impact
You can move from raw inputs to polished, customer‑specific content faster, keep materials aligned to your brand and narrative, and tailor messages to stakeholder needs with less manual effort. Content creation becomes a coordinated flow across change strategy, communication, and execution—supporting momentum throughout the program.

Getting Started
Collect a small set of canonical inputs (brand guidelines, message house, workshop notes, key slides) and define master prompts and templates for each asset type. Pilot with two priority deliverables—such as a vision statement and a stakeholder briefing—keep a human review step for accuracy and tone and expand to additional channels once the workflow proves reliable.

 

2nd Opportunity – Realizing appealing communication with tight budgets 

Scenario
The plan is solid, the milestones are set—but the budget for agencies and production support is too expensive. You still need a steady cadence of videos, posts, articles, and emails that land with different audiences and regions. The choice shouldn’t be “go quiet” or “burn out the team.”

AI Solution
Use generative AI to transform SAP standard content and your project artifacts (charters, release notes, FAQs, town‑hall transcripts) into ready‑to‑use assets—short explainer videos, community posts, newsletters, and stakeholder emails. Apply brand and tone guardrails, translate or reuse for key markets, and generate channel‑specific variants with approvals routed to your team.

Real Impact
You can maintain a consistent communications drumbeat without additional headcount, reach more audiences in their preferred channels and languages, and keep messages aligned to project milestones—preserving quality while staying within budget.

Getting Started
Identify your source content and priority channels, define brand/voice prompts and approval rules, and pilot two to three asset types (e.g., a monthly newsletter, a short video, and a stakeholder email). Keep human review for accuracy and context, then expand once the workflow feels reliable.

 

3rd Opportunity – Engage AI as your OCM sparring partner

Scenario
You’ve got strong change ideas, but not always the time—or the bench—to test them, iterate fast, and pressure‑test assumptions. Workshops end, momentum fades, and decisions wait for the next availability slot. You need a trusted counterpart that can challenge thinking and turn concepts into actionable plans on demand.

AI Solution
Set up an AI assistant grounded in your internal playbooks, past project lessons, and SAP good practices. Use it to iterate on change narratives, draft stakeholder maps and engagement plans, compare scenario options, summarize feedback patterns, and propose next‑best actions—available on demand, 24/7.

Real Impact
You can move from idea to plan faster, explore more options before committing, and document the rationale behind decisions. The assistant augments your expertise with rapid synthesis and structured recommendations, while you retain judgment and sign‑off.

Getting Started
Curate a safe, high‑quality knowledge base (frameworks, project docs, templates, lessons learned), define clear prompts and review criteria, and begin with co‑drafting tasks such as sponsor briefings or comms outlines. Validate outputs with your team, then extend to scenario testing and decision support as confidence grows.

Looking ahead

AI brings new momentum to how we deliver change—but its impact depends on how intentionally we choose to work with it. Start small with a clearly scoped use case, define success metrics, and iterate based on evidence. Keep experts in the loop throughout: AI accelerates first drafts, clustering, and signal detection, while humans ensure narrative, nuance, and sound decision‑making. Establish privacy, security, and responsible‑AI guardrails from day one to build trust and transparency.

But most importantly: don’t wait for “perfect AI.”
Experiment, learn, adapt—and share your experiences. Your insights help the wider change community move forward faster.

✓ Start with ONE high-pain use case (e.g., stakeholder analysis automation)

✓ Define success metrics upfront (time saved, quality scores, adoption lift)

✓ Keep humans in the loop: AI drafts, experts review and decide

✓ Establish guardrails: privacy, security, bias checks—non-negotiable from day one

We should not be waiting for perfect AI—keep experimenting, learning, and iterating.

If you want to go deeper or need support setting up your first AI‑powered OCM workflow, reach out via maja.polke@gmail.com or hannah.bruelke@gmail.com or explore our SharePoint site for templates, guides, and examples.

Together, let’s shape how AI elevates OCM—today and for the future.

entry.title: SAP Functional take on the real bottleneck - Who will train the bots?
entry.content:
If you work as an SAP functional consultant and also have a broader interest in technology, systems, and how things connect across domains, this perspective may be relevant to you. Read on.
SAP AI core provisioned us to BYOM(Bring your own model) concept. But to take advantage of local(own model), context rich, scalable, cost effective( remember last API token bill!) model, we also need to cleanse data, plot data and train the model. This blog discusses about feature engineering and mathematics behind feature selection in training ANN( artificial neural network) models using
Tensor flow playground as an example.

Prelude : There is a popular narrative forming. America builds the large language models.
China builds the robots. But this framing misses the harder problem. Someone still has to train these systems for real work.

Business case: A hospital chain in Ireland wants to replace human operators who answer incoming calls. The idea sounds straightforward: feed 10 years of call recordings into an AI system and automate the job. In reality, this is where the real effort begins.

Raw data is messy. / Call logs are inconsistent. / Audio quality varies. / Context is missing.
Sensitive information needs to be handled carefully.

Before any model training happens, there is a long pipeline:

* Cleaning and filtering unusable data
* Transcribing audio accurately
* Structuring conversations into usable formats
* Labelling intent, urgency, and outcomes
* Handling compliance and privacy constraints

Even then, training is not a one-time task.
The system needs continuous feedback, correction, and retraining to match real-world expectations.


Few notes if you are a polymath:

Tensor flow is machine learning framework provided by Google. A colleague challenged me to train a model on a given dataset with a clear objective: achieve the lowest possible test loss using the most efficient number of epochs.

Practical use case -

• Credit risk scoring

• Sales forecasting

Training Process Parameters

Epoch

  • What it is: One complete forward and backward pass of all training data through the network.

  • When to use: Tune to control training duration. Stop when validation loss stops improving (Early Stopping).

  • How to remember: Reading a textbook. 1 Epoch = reading the entire book cover-to-cover once.

Learning Rate (e.g., 0.03)

  • What it is: The step size the optimizer takes to update weights during gradient descent.

  • When to use: Adjust if training fails. High LR = fast but unstable (loss spikes). Low LR = stable but slow (gets stuck). 0.03 is a standard, moderate starting point.

  • How to remember: Mini-golf putt. High LR is a full swing (overshoots the hole). Low LR is a gentle tap (takes forever to reach).

Batch Size (e.g., 10)

  • What it is: The number of data samples processed before the model updates its internal weights.

  • When to use: Smaller batches (e.g., 10) add noise to the learning path, helping escape local minima but computing slower. Larger batches compute faster but require more memory.

  • How to remember: Carrying groceries. Do you update your trip carrying 1 item at a time, 10 items (mini-batch), or all at once (full batch)?

Model Architecture

Activation (e.g., Tanh)

  • What it is: A mathematical function attached to a neuron that decides whether it should "fire," introducing non-linearity to learn complex patterns.

  • When to use: Tanh is useful for hidden layers when zero-centered outputs are needed. ReLU is the modern default for deep networks.

  • How to remember: A club bouncer. Determines who gets to enter inside.(next layer pass filter)

Hidden Layers

  • What it is: The layers of mathematical operations between input features and final output. They extract increasingly complex patterns.

  • When to use: Add more layers/neurons when the model cannot capture the complexity of the data (underfitting). Reduce them if the model memorizes data (overfitting).

  • How to remember: An assembly line. Layer 1 detects edges, Layer 2 combines edges into shapes, Layer 3 identifies the object.

Regularization (L1 / L2)

  • What it is: A penalty added to the loss function to discourage the model from learning overly complex, extreme weights (prevents overfitting).

    • When to use: Apply when training loss is very low, but test loss is high.

    • How to remember: A complexity tax. Forces the model to find the simplest possible rule to separate the data rather than drawing crazy, intricate boundaries.

    Regularization Rate

    • What it is: The hyperparameter controlling the strength of the regularization penalty.

    • When to use: Tune alongside Regularization. Start small (0.001). Too high = model learns nothing.

    • How to remember: The specific tax percentage.

      Data & Evaluation

      Data

      • What it is: The underlying pattern or distribution of the raw dataset (e.g., circles, spirals, XOR).

      • When to use: Select different data shapes to test how well a specific architecture handles non-linear boundaries.

      Ratio of training to test data (e.g., 50%)

      • What it is: The split between data used to update weights (Train) and data held back to evaluate generalization (Test).

      • When to use: 80/20 is enterprise standard. 50/50 is used in TF Playground to artificially limit training data, forcing the model to work harder and highlighting overfitting quickly.

      • How to remember: Practice exams vs. Final Exam.

      Noise (e.g., 10)

      • What it is: Random variance or incorrect labels injected into the dataset.

      • When to use: Increase noise to simulate messy, real-world data and test model robustness.

      • How to remember: Listening to someone in a crowded room. Can the model find the signal despite the static?

      Features

      • What it is: The specific input representations     fed into the first layer.

      • When to use: Use feature engineering to help a simple network solve a complex geometric problem without adding hidden layers.

      • How to remember: House price prediction variables (Square footage, ZIP code, Age).

      Output: Test loss / Training loss

      • What it is: A numerical metric of how wrong the model's predictions are. Training loss evaluates seen data; Test loss evaluates unseen data.

      • Formula (Mean Squared Error):     Loss                        

      • When to use: Monitor continuously. If Training loss drops but Test loss rises, stop training (overfitting).

      • How to remember: Training loss = score on homework. Test loss = score on the final exam.

      Discretize Output

      • What it is: Forcing the continuous probability map (soft gradients) into rigid classification boundaries (solid colors).

      • When to use: Enable when you need to see the exact decision boundary the model has drawn, rather than its confidence levels.

      • How to remember: Rounding a grade. 89.5% becomes a discrete "A" rather than a continuous decimal.

Feature engineering -Mathematics behind model training- my learning!

  • A neural network is a computational model inspired by the human brain that consists of interconnected nodes, called neurons, which are organized in layers. The nodes are connected to the nodes in the next layer, creating a network. The first layer is the input layer, followed by hidden layers, and the last layer is the output layer.

Challenge 1-  Circular data set & a classification problem type- Target- achieve Test loss less eq. 0.003

( Refer notes above -that is more important than training loss)- landing page - before model training setup change

Go to below url to try it out yourselves. STOP reading if you want to solve yourself since you now understand each parameter !

A Neural Network Playground

How to solve?

The key is to understand the dataset first, and then tune the model accordingly.
