For example, when working with a circular dataset, feature selection should be guided by the underlying geometry. We must remember formula of circle x2   + y2    = r 2

Hence in below solution feature introduced accordingly, activation Tanh used for centered output. Fine tune learning rate, Regularization and Reg. Rate. Important note ratio of training to test data chosen as 70%,batch size 15- however you could try with increasing noise in the dataset and yet tune the model to achieve similar result. Minimum Epoch count is desired and we strive to avoid 'overfitting' and under fitting of training data to the model. Final setup below-

Challenge 2-Spiral data set & a classification problem type- Target- achieve Test loss less eq. 0.003
Note- If I keep above settings for training, model does not work at all- the Epocs( training run time) rises but 'Test loss' does not reduce.
How to solve- analysis and inference

Retail context

  • Circle dataset represent = Purely Seasonal (same every year).
  • Spiral dataset represent = Seasonal + Growth/Trend.

Following similar analogy of circular data set. I have now solved spiral dataset as well.
It is tied with mathematics again.

Unlike circular dataset ,here formula is not x2   + y2    = r 2

a Spiral is defined by a radius that changes in proportion to the angle 

Next the main trick ,for circular dataset, I was  using below features

These features only tell the model "how far" a point is from the centre, but they completely ignore the angle. For a spiral, two points can have the same distance from the centre but belong to different arms because their angles are different.

To solve the spiral efficiently (low epoch count), you need features that help the model understand Rotation.

So we shall choose Sin(x) function to add the periodicity (repeating) flavour.

If the Playground allowed it, the ideal feature would be

but since it doesn't, we  rely on increased network depth. I have used gradual decreasing neurons per layer.(Tapered approach, so that model generalise to new data)

Final setup shown below-

Next - You could try to increase Noise parameter to 10 to simulate dirty data common in SAP system.
also could try another round of optimisation minimalist architect's approach by reducing hidden layer to 3 and increasing Neuron count slightly in the middle.

Challenge 3- Gaussian pattern dataset& a classification problem type-

Target- achieve Test loss less eq. 0.001

How to solve - Setup 1

Setup 2(Note Epoch increased compared to 1st trial)

 

Setup-3 - Note Test loss is zero in each case but Epoch is still not optimised!

Maths Alert! - we might ask - Why X1 and X2 feature are sufficient for training gaussian data set where mathematically it looks for mean value and complex formula?

Being pragmatic- Linear separability: The Gaussian dataset shown consists of two distinct clusters. Because they are separated in space, a simple straight line is enough to divide the orange class from the blue class.
Hence,X1 and X2 are sufficient because the neural network is performing binary classification, not density estimation. It does not need to learn or replicate the complex Gaussian probability formula; it only needs to draw a straight line between the two clusters.

Experiment never stopped!

Setup 4 - FINAL -note BATCH SIZE = 1

This is called "Stochastic Gradient Descent." The model will update its weights after every single run, allowing it to find the boundary line much faster in terms of epochs.

 

Real world Stress test - Next trial - increase the noise to 30 and see the model behaviour

 

Challenge 4- XOR dataset & a classification problem type

Final setup : Note no hidden layer(neurons) yet target of Training loss achieved.

Maths rationale:  

The classic XOR dataset consists of four quadrants that cannot be separated by drawing a single straight line using raw X1 and X2 inputs. By using the product X1 X2 as the sole feature, the math resolves the problem before training even begins:

  • Top-Right Quadrant: X1 is (+), X2 is (+)  --> (+ x +) = Positive

  • Bottom-Left Quadrant: X1  is (-), X2 is (-) -->  (- x -) = Positive

  • Top-Left Quadrant: X1 is (-), X2 is (+) --> (- x +) = Negative

  • Bottom-Right Quadrant: X1 is (+), X2 is (-)  -->  (+ x -) = Negative


Food for thought : In modern enterprise AI systems, hand-crafting polynomial interactions across thousands of features is impossible. It is a bad idea to design architectures relying on this. To build scalable models, you must pass raw features into hidden layers with non-linear activations (like ReLU) so the network automatically learns these complex cross-feature interactions.

Conclusion: (Pls ignore formatting issues )Try out the fun yourself!  A Neural Network Playground

Left- Regression problems for you! - We try to predict a continuously changing output - it is not categorical also read more about:  1/ SGD (Stochastic Gradient Descent) 2/ ADAM (Adaptive Moment Estimation) as efficient optimization algorithm.

entry.title: SAP RPT-1 Meets Employee Attrition: A Hands-On Test with SuccessFactors Data
entry.content:
The Short Version

I wanted to see if SAP’s new tabular AI model, RPT-1, could predict which employees are likely to quit, using nothing but a CSV extracted from SuccessFactors fields. No model training. No data science team. Just upload and predict.

To keep things honest, I based the experiment on a real peer-reviewed study that used actual employee data and traditional ML. The result? RPT-1 nailed all 5 test predictions in under 400 milliseconds. But the full story is more interesting than the headline.

 

Why I Picked Attrition (and Not Another Sales Demo)

Credit where it's due: Hari Panakkal's recent SAP Community post, Experiment with SAP RPT-1: Predicting the Right Carrier in SAP TM Using the No-Code Playground, is a great walkthrough of RPT-1 on transportation data. It got me thinking: if RPT-1 works that well for carrier prediction in TM, what happens when you point it at an HR problem?

Most RPT-1 demos I've seen focus on sales forecasting or logistics. Those are solid use cases, but the one I care about is people analytics, and the single biggest question HR teams ask is: "Who's about to leave?"

Attrition is a natural fit for RPT-1 because:

  • The data is tabular and already sitting in SuccessFactors
  • The problem is binary classification (stays vs. leaves)
  • Most HR teams don't have ML engineers on staff

But I didn't want to just throw random HR data at it. I wanted to test RPT-1 against something with a known answer.

 

The Research Behind the Experiment

I grounded everything in this study:

Manafi Varkiani, S., Pattarin, F., Fabbri, T., & Fantoni, G. (2025). Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants. Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.

Here’s what makes this paper worth paying attention to:

It used real data. Not overused Kaggle dataset, but actual records from an Italian financial company. 5,767 employees. 309 voluntary resignations (a 5.4% attrition rate, which is realistic and makes the problem genuinely hard).

They tested four algorithms head-to-head. Random Forest crushed everything: 99.9% AUC, 94.4% sensitivity. Logistic Regression and Decision Trees weren’t even close.

They found things that don’t match your intuition. This is the best part. Using SHAP analysis, they discovered that employees who received bonuses were more likely to leave (burnout from overperforming), and employees who got retention incentives also showed higher attrition. It turns out retention incentives are targeted at people already flagged as flight risks, so the incentive is a signal of existing risk, not a cause of it.

The ML pipeline was serious work. ROSE resampling for class imbalance, 10-fold cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning, and SHAP post-processing. This took significant data science effort.

So the question became: Can RPT-1 get comparable results with a single API call?

 

Mapping the Variables to SuccessFactors

The study used 14 employee attributes. I mapped each one to the actual OData entity and field in SuccessFactors, and validated every mapping against the live API:

 

Research Variable

Where It Lives in SF

Category

Gender

PerPersonal.gender

Personal

Age Class

Derived from PerPerson.dateOfBirth

Personal

Tenure Class

Derived from EmpEmployment.startDate

People

Department

EmpJob.department

Work Environment

Rank

EmpJob.payGrade → FOPayGrade (grouped)

Management

Contract Type

EmpJob.contractType

Management

Job Level

EmpJob.payGrade

Management

Talent Program

TalentPool membership

People

Bonus Received

EmpPayCompNonRecurring existence

Management

Hours (PT/FT)

EmpJob.standardHours

Management

Retention Incentive

Custom MDF or EmpPayCompRecurring

Management

Job Family

FOJobCode.jobFunction

Management

Log Salary

EmpPayCompRecurring.paycompvalue (log10)

Management

Log Premium

Performance bonus (log10)

Management

Picture 1: Ishikawa – Fishbone diagram

 

What Is RPT-1, Exactly?

SAP RPT-1 is a Relational Pretrained Transformer , a foundation model built for tabular data, not text. Announced at TechEd 2025, it comes in three flavors:

  • sap-rpt-1-small : Fast commercial model (low latency)
  • sap-rpt-1-large : Higher accuracy commercial model
  • sap-rpt-1-oss : Open-source on GitHub (Apache 2.0)

The key idea: you give it labeled rows as examples and rows with [PREDICT] placeholders as questions. It learns from the context: no training, no feature engineering, no resampling. Think of it like few-shot prompting in ChatGPT, but for spreadsheets.

 

Running the Experiment

Step 1: Build the Dataset

I created sf_employee_attrition.csv with 50 labeled employee records using the 14 features from the research, plus 5 query rows with [PREDICT] in the ATTRITION column. The labeled data reflects the patterns the study found: higher attrition among younger, shorter-tenure employees with lower compensation, and the counterintuitive bonus/retention effects.

 

Picture 2 - sf_employee_attrition.csv, 50 labeled + 5 query rows, 14 features mapped to SF fields

 

Step 2: The No-Code Route (Playground)

Easiest way to test. Go to rpt.cloud.sap/dashboard:

  1. Upload the CSV
  2. The interface detects the [PREDICT] cells
  3. Click Predict
  4. Done

No coding. Supports up to 2,073 rows and 50 columns.

 

Step 3: The API Route (for Pipelines)

This option allows full control and evaluation metrics:

"""
SAP RPT-1 Employee Attrition Prediction
========================================
This script demonstrates how to use SAP-RPT-1 (Relational Pretrained Transformer)
to predict employee attrition using structured HR data modeled after
SuccessFactors workforce analytics dimensions.

Dataset design informed by: Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025),
"Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants",
Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.


Prerequisites:
  - For API approach: SAP RPT Playground account (https://rpt.cloud.sap)
  - For OSS approach: Python 3.11+, GPU with 24-80GB VRAM recommended

Author: Shakti Barath
"""

import json
import csv
import requests
import os
from pathlib import Path

# ============================================================
# CONFIGURATION
# ============================================================

# Option A: Load token from file (recommended)
# Create access_token.json with: {"token": "YOUR_TOKEN_HERE"}
# Get your token from: https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs (scroll to bottom)
TOKEN_FILE = "access_token.json"

# Option B: Set as environment variable
# export SAP_RPT_TOKEN="your_token_here"

RPT_API_URL = "https://rpt.cloud.sap/api/predict"
CSV_FILE = "sf_employee_attrition.csv"
TARGET_COLUMN = "ATTRITION"
PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER = "[PREDICT]"

def load_token():
    """Load API token from file or environment variable."""
    if os.path.exists(TOKEN_FILE):
        with open(TOKEN_FILE) as f:
            data = json.load(f)
            return data.get("token", "")
    return os.environ.get("SAP_RPT_TOKEN", "")

def load_csv_as_rows(filepath):
    """
    Load CSV and convert to RPT-1 row format.
    
    RPT-1 expects rows as a list of dicts. Rows with [PREDICT] in the 
    target column serve as query rows; all others are context rows
    (few-shot examples for in-context learning).
    """
    rows = []
    with open(filepath, newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        for row in reader:
            rows.append(dict(row))
    return rows

def build_payload(rows, target_column, index_column="EMP_ID"):
    """
    Build the RPT-1 API request payload.
    
    Structure:
    - rows: list of dicts (context + query rows)
    - index_column: unique identifier column
    - prediction_config: specifies target and task type
    
    Query rows contain [PREDICT] in the target column.
    Context rows contain actual labels — these are the few-shot examples.
    """
    payload = {
        "rows": rows,
        "index_column": index_column,
        "prediction_config": {
            "target_columns": [
                {
                    "name": target_column,
                    "placeholder_value": PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER,
                    "task_type": "classification"
                }
            ]
        }
    }
    return payload

def call_rpt1_api(payload, token):
    """Send prediction request to SAP RPT-1 Playground API."""
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "Accept": "application/json"
    }
    
    print("Sending prediction request to SAP RPT-1...")
    print(f"  Context rows: {sum(1 for r in payload['rows'] if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) != PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER)}")
    print(f"  Query rows:   {sum(1 for r in payload['rows'] if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) == PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER)}")
    
    response = requests.post(RPT_API_URL, headers=headers, json=payload, timeout=120)
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response.json()

def display_predictions(result, rows):
    """Display RPT-1 predictions alongside employee context."""
    predictions = result.get("prediction", {}).get("predictions", [])
    
    # Build lookup for employee context
    emp_lookup = {str(r["EMP_ID"]): r for r in rows}
    
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)
    print("SAP RPT-1 ATTRITION PREDICTIONS")
    print("=" * 80)
    
    for pred in predictions:
        emp_id = str(pred.get("id", ""))
        emp = emp_lookup.get(emp_id, {})
        attrition_pred = pred.get(TARGET_COLUMN, [{}])
        
        if isinstance(attrition_pred, list) and len(attrition_pred) > 0:
            predicted_value = attrition_pred[0].get("prediction", "N/A")
            confidence = attrition_pred[0].get("confidence")
        else:
            predicted_value = "N/A"
            confidence = None
        
        risk_indicator = "HIGH RISK" if predicted_value == "Yes" else "LOW RISK"
        
        print(f"\n  Employee {emp_id}: {emp.get('JOB_FAMILY', 'Unknown')}")
        print(f"  Department: {emp.get('DEPARTMENT', '-')} | "
              f"Age: {emp.get('AGE_CLASS', '-')} | "
              f"Tenure: {emp.get('TENURE_CLASS', '-')}")
        print(f"  Talent Program: {emp.get('TALENT_PROGRAM', '-')} | "
              f"Contract: {emp.get('CONTRACT_TYPE', '-')}")
        print(f"  Prediction: {predicted_value} ({risk_indicator})")
        if confidence is not None:
            print(f"  Confidence: {confidence:.2%}")
    
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)

def analyze_context_patterns(rows):
    """
    Analyze attrition patterns in context data.
    Mirrors the descriptive statistics approach from the research paper
    (Manafi Varkiani et al., 2025, Table 6).
    """
    context_rows = [r for r in rows if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) != PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER]
    
    print("\n" + "-" * 60)
    print("CONTEXT DATA ANALYSIS (mirrors research methodology)")
    print("-" * 60)
    
    # Attrition by age class
    age_stats = {}
    for r in context_rows:
        age = r["AGE_CLASS"]
        left = r[TARGET_COLUMN] == "Yes"
        if age not in age_stats:
            age_stats[age] = {"total": 0, "left": 0}
        age_stats[age]["total"] += 1
        age_stats[age]["left"] += int(left)
    
    print("\nAttrition Rate by Age Class:")
    for age in sorted(age_stats.keys()):
        s = age_stats[age]
        rate = (s["left"] / s["total"] * 100) if s["total"] > 0 else 0
        print(f"  {age:12s}: {rate:5.1f}%  ({s['left']}/{s['total']})")
    
    # Attrition by tenure class
    tenure_stats = {}
    for r in context_rows:
        tenure = r["TENURE_CLASS"]
        left = r[TARGET_COLUMN] == "Yes"
        if tenure not in tenure_stats:
            tenure_stats[tenure] = {"total": 0, "left": 0}
        tenure_stats[tenure]["total"] += 1
        tenure_stats[tenure]["left"] += int(left)
    
    print("\nAttrition Rate by Tenure Class:")
    for tenure in sorted(tenure_stats.keys()):
        s = tenure_stats[tenure]
        rate = (s["left"] / s["total"] * 100) if s["total"] > 0 else 0
        print(f"  {tenure:12s}: {rate:5.1f}%  ({s['left']}/{s['total']})")
    
    # Key insight from the research
    print("\n  Key Insight (Manafi Varkiani et al., 2025):")
    print("  Younger employees and those with shorter tenure show")
    print("  significantly higher attrition rates. The research found")
    print("  15.9% attrition for age 18-25 vs 1.4% for age 50-65.")

# ============================================================
# MAIN EXECUTION
# ============================================================

def main():
    print("=" * 80)
    print("SAP RPT-1: Employee Attrition Prediction Experiment")
    print("Grounded in: Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025), ESWA 272")
    print("=" * 80)
    
    # Load data
    if not Path(CSV_FILE).exists():
        print(f"ERROR: {CSV_FILE} not found. Place it in the same directory.")
        return
    
    rows = load_csv_as_rows(CSV_FILE)
    print(f"\nLoaded {len(rows)} employee records from {CSV_FILE}")
    
    # Analyze context patterns
    analyze_context_patterns(rows)
    
    # Load token
    token = load_token()
    if not token:
        print("\n" + "-" * 60)
        print("NO API TOKEN FOUND")
        print("-" * 60)
        print("To run predictions with the RPT Playground API:")
        print("1. Sign up at https://rpt.cloud.sap")
        print("2. Copy your token from https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs")
        print("3. Save it to access_token.json:")
        print('   {"token": "YOUR_TOKEN_HERE"}')
        print("\nAlternatively, use the OSS version (see rpt1_oss_predict.py)")
        return
    
    # Build payload and predict
    payload = build_payload(rows, TARGET_COLUMN)
    
    try:
        result = call_rpt1_api(payload, token)
        display_predictions(result, rows)
        
        # Save raw response
        with open("rpt1_predictions.json", "w") as f:
            json.dump(result, f, indent=2)
        print(f"\nRaw predictions saved to rpt1_predictions.json")
        
    except requests.exceptions.HTTPError as e:
        print(f"\nAPI Error: {e}")
        print("Check your token and ensure the playground is accessible.")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"\nError: {e}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Create access_token.json in the same folder with your RPT-1 API key:

{
  "token": "YOUR_RPT-API_KEY"
}

 

The Results

I ran the CSV through the RPT Playground API. Here’s exactly what came back:

EMP ID

Profile

Prediction

Confidence

Why It Makes Sense

1051

Finance • Staff • Permanent

Yes

95%

Age 25–30, Tenure 0–3 yr, Talent Program, Low premium

1052

IT • Staff • Permanent

No

98%

Age 30–40, Tenure 10–20 yr, Bonus received, Strong salary

1053

Sales • Staff • Fixed-Term

Yes

99%

Age 18–25, Tenure 0–3 yr, Fixed-term, Lowest salary

1054

HR • Manager • Permanent

No

99%

Age 40–50, Tenure 10–20 yr, Manager rank, High comp

1055

Ops • Staff • Permanent

Yes

94%

Age 25–30, Talent Prog, Retention incentive, Lower salary

 

Accuracy

Avg Confidence

Inference Time

Context Rows

5/5 (100%)

97%

399 ms

50 labeled rows

 

What Stood Out

The easy calls were easy. Employee 1053 (young, short-tenure, fixed-term, lowest salary) and Employee 1054 (senior manager, long tenure, high pay) both got 99% confidence. These are the extremes, exactly where the research paper’s Random Forest also showed the clearest separation.

Employee 1055 was the interesting one. This person has moderate tenure (3–10 years), which in the training data strongly correlates with staying. But RPT-1 correctly flagged them as at-risk at 94% confidence. Why? The combination of a retention incentive, talent program membership, and lower compensation. Remember what the research found: retention incentives are a signal that someone is already a flight risk, not a shield against it. RPT-1 picked up that interaction from just 50 context rows. That’s impressive.

399 milliseconds. The research paper required ROSE resampling, 10-fold cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning across four algorithms, and SHAP post-processing. RPT-1 got directionally equivalent predictions with a single API call on raw CSV data. For an HR team that wants a quick risk screening before committing to a full ML build, that’s a big deal.

The honest caveat: This is a 50-row synthetic dataset with intentionally clear patterns. Production data with 5,767 employees and a 5.4% attrition rate would be a harder test. These results show RPT-1 can read tabular patterns well. A head-to-head on real production data is the next step.

 

What RPT-1 Is Good At Here

  • No ML pipeline required. The research team spent serious effort on resampling, cross-validation, and algorithm selection. RPT-1 skips all of that.
  • Handles mixed data out of the box. Categorical and numerical fields go in as-is. No encoding needed.
  • Tolerates missing data. The research had to drop 5 variables due to gaps. RPT-1 handles missing values by design.
  • Easy to iterate. Want to add engagement survey scores? Just add a column. No pipeline rebuild.

 

What RPT-1 Can’t Do (Yet)

  • No explainability. RPT-1 gives you a prediction and a confidence score, but not why. For HR decisions, that matters a lot.
  • Class imbalance is your problem. With a 5.4% attrition rate, your context rows need careful curation.
  • No time-series awareness. RPT-1 works on static snapshots. It can’t predict when someone will leave.
  • Playground has limits. 2,073 rows max. For production scale, deploy via SAP AI Core.

 

So What Should You Actually Do With This?

RPT-1 makes it trivially easy to generate attrition predictions from SuccessFactors data. But predictions without understanding can lead to bad interventions. (Remember the retention incentive example: a naïve reading suggests incentives cause attrition.)

My recommendation:

  1. Use RPT-1 as a rapid screening tool. Upload your SF extract, get a risk score, identify who needs a closer look.
  2. Build a traditional ML model in parallel for explainability. Run the same features through Random Forest with SHAP to understand the why.
  3. Loop in HR. Especially for counterintuitive patterns. Data literacy plus domain knowledge turns a prediction into an action.
  4. Don’t treat aggregate results as universal. Attrition drivers for one job family can differ completely from the company-wide pattern.

 

References

  • Manafi Varkiani, S., Pattarin, F., Fabbri, T., & Fantoni, G. (2025). Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants. Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.
  • Lundberg, S. M., & Lee, S. I. (2017). A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions. NeurIPS 2017.
  • SAP-RPT-1: sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/sap-rpt.html
  • SAP-RPT-1-OSS: github.com/SAP-samples/sap-rpt-1-oss
  • RPT Playground: rpt.cloud.sap
  • Reference Blog — Carrier Prediction in SAP TM: SAP Community (Mar 2026)

Happy experimenting!

Built with synthetic data mirroring the variable structure and patterns from Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025). No real employee data was used. Results are illustrative.

entry.title: Exploring Model Lifecycle Management with Python Machine Learning Client for SAP HANA
entry.content:
1. Introduction

This blog post introduces key model lifecycle management features in the SAP HANA Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) through the Python Machine Learning Client for SAP HANA (hana_ml). PAL provides rich in-database machine learning capabilities, while hana_ml exposes these capabilities in Python so that users can build, execute, and operate model workflows directly on SAP HANA data with minimal data movement.

Model lifecycle development usually starts from a fit task, followed by some scoring tasks to evaluate the performance of a derived model. After repeated cycles of fitting and scoring tasks to get an acceptable model, the model can be delivered for continual prediction tasks and optional drift detection tasks. Drift detection is used to answer the question of whether the currently deployed model is still performing acceptably, since patterns in prediction data may change from time to time. Once drift is confirmed, a fit task on the latest data is required, and therefore a new cycle of model development begins.

Experiment tracking is a feature that automatically traces and persists some information for the input and output of PAL procedure execution. Each PAL procedure call is identified by a tracking ID. The tracking ID consists of two parts: an experiment ID and a run ID, representing one experiment and one run respectively. Logically, the relationship between experiment and run is one-to-many. One experiment usually targets a single task such as fitting, predicting, or scoring, with a selected algorithm. Users may have multiple runs for the same experiment while exploring different hyperparameters and/or datasets.

The persisted information for experiment tracking consists of two parts: tracking metadata and tracking logs. Different APIs are available to retrieve them. Tracking metadata records information about the invoked PAL procedure and its current running status. Tracking logs are persisted in chronological order, each of them has a log type, log value and log timestamp.

The available log types include:

  • Parameter: PAL configuration for each procedure. This is essential information for rerunning the procedure.
  • Dataset Metadata: Metadata of the input data, including the dataset source and descriptions of the fields in the dataset.
  • Model Signature: Information about how to use the model, including model input data format, accepted configuration parameters, and output data structure.
  • Metric: Metric data that records statistical information for the model.
  • Figure: Discrete and continuous figure data used for plotting and analysis.

Reproducing the exact same result with a given dataset and configuration is a fundamental requirement in model development. It can be used for validating findings, debugging models, and ensuring consistent behavior over time. The logs of input parameters, dataset metadata, and tracking metadata are essential information for achieving this purpose.

The model signature log can guide users in executing prediction and scoring tasks with a derived model. Metric and figure logs can be used for analyzing model performance.

Model Storage is another important feature for model development. Users can identify a model by model name and model version. Model storage serves as the standard source for machine learning models when committing prediction, scoring and evaluation tasks. Besides, newly generated models can be pushed into model storage with a selected model name and model version.

Drift detection is a turning point in model development and runtime operations. It alerts users that model degradation may happen or has happened. This can be achieved with the features of drift detection combined with metric and/or figure track entities described above. Currently hana_ml provides visual charts to compare metrics between different experiment runs to verify whether such a drift has happened.

Finally, automation drives the workflow across model development activities. With the Scheduled Execution feature, users can not only automate single tasks such as model fit, model prediction, and model scoring, but also orchestrate related tasks in a defined order or in parallel. All automated tasks can be scheduled as background jobs either immediately or by time-frequency settings.

With all these features in hana_ml, users can construct flexible model development workflows, including model fitting, model testing, model storage, model serving, and drift detection.

 

2. Use Case: End-to-End Model Lifecycle Management

This use case shows a compact end-to-end model lifecycle workflow based on the public Pima Indians Diabetes dataset. This example is intended for demonstration and teaching purposes only. It uses SAP HANA Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) together with the Python Machine Learning Client for SAP HANA (hana_ml) to illustrate how a model is trained, tracked, stored, operationalized, and monitored over time. The notebook example for this use case can be downloaded from the ml-lifecycle-examples folder.

Lifecycle phases covered:

  • Baseline Training & Experiment Tracking:​ Train an initial model and log all parameters, metrics, and artifacts.
  • Model Storage & Management:​ Save, version, load, and manage the model.
  • Scheduled Inference:​ Automate predictions on a regular schedule.
  • Monitoring & Retraining:​ Simulate monitoring for data drift and retraining decisions.

Dataset note: this example uses the Pima Indians Diabetes dataset, a binary classification task based on clinical measurements such as glucose concentration, BMI, and age.

Step 0: Environment and Data Preparation

We start by importing the core libraries and creating a connection to SAP HANA. This connection context is the entry point for loading data, running PAL algorithms, and managing lifecycle artifacts in the database. It is the required first step because all later actions reuse it.

from hana_ml import dataframe
from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.utility import DataSets
from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.unified_classification import UnifiedClassification
from hana_ml.artifacts.tracking.tracking import MLExperiments, delete_experiment_log, get_tracking_log
from hana_ml.visualizers.tracking import ExperimentMonitor, ScheduledTaskMonitor
from hana_ml.model_storage import ModelStorage

# Establish a connection to SAP HANA
conn = dataframe.ConnectionContext(url='<host>', port=<port>, user='<user>', password='<pwd>')

Replace ‘host’, ‘port’, ‘user’, and ‘pwd’ with your SAP HANA instance details.

Next, use train_test_val_split to create the main working subsets: df_train for baseline fitting, df_score for holdout evaluation, and df_inference for scheduled batch prediction. This keeps training, evaluation, and operational inference clearly separated.

from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.partition import train_test_val_split
df_full, _, _, _ = DataSets.load_diabetes_data(conn)
df_train, df_score, _ = train_test_val_split(data=df_full, partition_method='random', random_seed=23, 
                                            training_percentage=0.8, testing_percentage=0.2, validation_percentage=0.0, id_column="ID")
# Save tables in HANA
df_train.save("PIMA_INDIANS_DIABETES_TRAIN_TBL")
df_score.save("PIMA_INDIANS_DIABETES_SCORE_TBL")

print("Train sample shape : ", df_train.shape)
print("The first 3 rows: ")
print(df_train.head(3).collect())

print("The first 3 rows of inference sample (no label column):")
df_inference = df_score.deselect("CLASS")
print(df_inference.head(3).collect())

Fig.1 Data samples

To mimic a simple monitoring scenario, we then construct weekly batches with different label distributions. These batches keep the label column so later scoring runs can be compared for drift.

# Simulated weekly batches for drift observation (different label mix).
week20_hdf = df_score.filter('ID < 100')
week21_hdf = df_score.filter('CLASS = 1')
week22_hdf = df_score.filter('CLASS = 0')

week20_hdf.save('DIABETES_WEEK20')
week21_hdf.save('DIABETES_WEEK21')
week22_hdf.save('DIABETES_WEEK22')

print(week20_hdf.shape)
print(week21_hdf.shape)
print(week22_hdf.shape)

Fig 2. Shape of simulated data

Step 1. Baseline Training and Experiment Tracking

This step establishes the initial reference model. We train a Hybrid Gradient Boosting Tree classifier, use MLExperiments to log parameters and metrics automatically, and keep the run history auditable. Training and tracking are created together.

We begin by creating a dedicated tracking session identified by a unique EXPERIMENT_ID. Within that experiment, training and scoring are recorded as separate runs.

# Constants for the workflow
EXPERIMENT_ID = "BLOG_DIABETES_TRACKING"
MODEL_NAME = "BLOG_DIAB_HGBT"
TASK_ID = "DIABETES_WEEKLY_PREDICT"

# Optional, clear previous tracking logs so each run starts from a clean state.
delete_experiment_log(conn, EXPERIMENT_ID)

# Initialize the experiment tracker
tracker = MLExperiments(
    connection_context=conn,
    experiment_id=EXPERIMENT_ID,
    experiment_description="diabetes experiment")

# Define the hyperparameter grid for model search.
param_values = {
    "learning_rate": [0.1, 0.4],
    "n_estimators": [5, 10],
    "split_threshold": [0.1, 0.3]}

We configure the model with grid search and cross-validation, then enable autologging. This captures the hyperparameters, source dataset, and generated artifacts under the run name “Diagnosis_classifier-fit”. The key value is reproducibility: the tracked baseline can later be compared with monitoring runs.

uhgbt = UnifiedClassification(
    func="HybridGradientBoostingTree",
    param_search_strategy="grid",
    resampling_method="cv",
    evaluation_metric="error_rate",
    ref_metric=["auc"],
    fold_num=5,
    random_state=123,
    param_values=param_values)

# Enable automatic tracking of parameters, metrics, and artifacts.
tracker.autologging(
    model=uhgbt,
    run_name="Diagnosis_classifier-fit",
    dataset_name="diabetes",
    dataset_source="PIMA_INDIANS_DIABETES_TRAIN_TBL")
# Train the model using stratified partitioning
uhgbt.fit(data=df_train, key="ID", label="CLASS", partition_method="stratified",
    partition_random_state=5, stratified_column="CLASS")

# Log a separate scoring run
tracker.autologging(
    model=uhgbt,
    run_name="Diagnosis_classifier-score",
    dataset_name="diabetes",
    dataset_source="PIMA_INDIANS_DIABETES_SCORE_TBL")
score_pred, score_stats, score_cm, score_metrics = uhgbt.score(
    data=df_score, key="ID", label="CLASS")

After execution, we can retrieve and inspect the tracking artifacts for the current run:

tracking_id = tracker.get_current_tracking_id()
print(f"tracking id: {tracking_id}")
print(tracker.get_tracking_metadata_for_current_run().collect())
print(get_tracking_log(connection_context=conn, tracking_id).head(5).collect())

Fig. 3 Tracking metadata and log

We also provide a dashboard view of tracked runs, metrics, and artifacts.

experiment_monitor = ExperimentMonitor(connection_context=conn, experiment_ids=[EXPERIMENT_ID])
experiment_monitor.start()

Fig. 4 Experiement dashboard

In the Experiment Monitor, the experiment named “BLOG_DIABETES_TRACKING” contains two runs. Opening a run shows its tracked details, including parameters, metrics, and visual artifacts. For example, the run labeled “Diagnosis_classifier-score” shows charts such as ROC and cumulative gains plots.

Fig.5 Continuous figure

Step 2. Model Storage & Management

In this step, we persist the selected model in SAP HANA. This creates a versioned model artifact that can be loaded for later use. The ModelStorage class handles save, list, load, and delete operations. In the notebook, this step shows how the tracked baseline is promoted into a reusable deployment artifact.

# Example decision: choose uhgbt as the baseline operational model.
candidate_model = uhgbt
# Assign model identity fields before persisting.
candidate_model.name = MODEL_NAME
candidate_model.version = 1
model_storage = ModelStorage(connection_context=conn)
# if_exists='replace' overwrites an existing model with the same name/version.
model_storage.save_model(model=candidate_model, if_exists="replace") 

# List the models
model_storage.list_models(name=MODEL_NAME)

deployed_model = model_storage.load_model(name=MODEL_NAME)
print(f"Deployed model from storage: {MODEL_NAME}")

Fig. 6 Model list

 

The following command is optional cleanup for demo artifacts.

model_storage.delete_models(name=MODEL_NAME)

Step 3. Operationalize with Scheduler

This step moves the stored model into an operational workflow by creating scheduled inference. The scheduler runs predictions on a defined cadence, such as weekly, without manual execution. In other words, deployment is not only about storing a model, but also about defining how it will run repeatedly.

# Import and initialize the scheduler
from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.scheduler import ScheduledExecution
sexec = ScheduledExecution(conn)

# Define a prediction task using the deployed model
sexec.create_predict_task(
    obj=deployed_model,
    predict_params={"data": df_inference, "key": "ID"},
    task_id=TASK_ID,
    force=True)

# Schedule the task to run automatically
weekly_cron = "* * * mon 8 0 0"
schedule_info = sexec.create_task_schedule(
    task_id=TASK_ID,
    cron=weekly_cron,
    force=True)
schedule_info.collect()

Fig.7 Schedule information

# Launch the scheduler monitoring dashboard
scheduled_task_monitor = ScheduledTaskMonitor(connection_context=conn, task_ids=[TASK_ID])
scheduled_task_monitor.start()

Fig.8 Scheduled Task Monitor

 

The following commands are included for reference only. In the notebook, they are optional inspection and validation utilities rather than required steps.

  • Query Schedule & Logs: The function query_task_schedule(task_id) returns two DataFrames. The first describes the schedule definition, such as the cron pattern, and the second contains execution logs for historical runs, which are useful for auditing and debugging.
  • Trigger a Manual Validation Run: The function create_one_off_task_schedule(task_id) triggers an immediate out-of-schedule execution of the task. This is useful for validating the setup before relying on the automated schedule.
  • Remove the Schedule: The function remove_task_schedule(task_id) removes the schedule binding for the specified task ID.

Step 4. Monitor Drift Signals and Decide Whether to Retrain

This final step closes the model lifecycle loop by simulating production monitoring. We reuse the weekly batches prepared earlier, score them with the deployed model, and compare whether the tracked metrics remain stable or start to drift. The main idea is not the exact metric values in this toy example, but the monitoring pattern: repeated scoring of later batches against the same deployed model.

# Optional
WEEKLY_EXPERIMENT_ID = "WEEKLY_HGBT_TRACK"
delete_experiment_log(conn, WEEKLY_EXPERIMENT_ID)

# Create a dedicated experiment for production-like weekly monitoring.
MLModel_weekly_tracking = MLExperiments(
    connection_context=conn,
    experiment_id=WEEKLY_EXPERIMENT_ID,
    experiment_description="Monitor of HGBT model weekly"
)

# Reuse the weekly slices prepared earlier and log one score run per week.
weekly_batches = [
    ("week20-score", week20_hdf, "diabetes_week20", "DIABETES_WEEK20"),
    ("week21-score", week21_hdf, "diabetes_week21", "DIABETES_WEEK21"),
    ("week22-score", week22_hdf, "diabetes_week22", "DIABETES_WEEK22"),
]

for run_name, weekly_batch, dataset_name, dataset_source in weekly_batches:
    MLModel_weekly_tracking.autologging(
        model=deployed_model,
        run_name=run_name,
        dataset_name=dataset_name,
        dataset_source=dataset_source
    )
    score_pred, score_stats, score_cm, score_metrics = deployed_model.score(
        weekly_batch,
        key="ID",
        label="CLASS"
    )

You can visualize these trends, such as accuracy or AUC, in the Experiment Monitor dashboard. For example, in the simulated scenarios for Weeks 20, 21, and 22, you can select the accuracy metric, choose the three corresponding weekly runs, and click Compare to open a detailed comparison view.

Fig. 9 Weekly monitor

In the figure below, you can observe fluctuations in accuracy across the weeks, for example from 0.78 to 0.61 and then to 0.81. A sharp shift such as the drop in Week 21 would be a reasonable drift signal. In a real workflow, that signal would trigger a review and could lead to actions such as retraining the model.

Fig. 10 Model Drift

3. Summary

This blog post shows the key features and an end-to-end model lifecycle workflow use case with SAP HANA PAL and hana_ml. Conceptually, model lifecycle management in SAP HANA PAL with hana_ml is a closed-loop process that connects model development, model registration, model operations, and post-deployment monitoring in one traceable workflow. In this article, we use a compact scenario to explain that progression from baseline training to operational monitoring.

References

Notebook download folder: ml-lifecycle-examples

Product Documentation:

  • PAL: Tracking
  • hana_ml: Track
  • PAL: Schedule
  • hana_ml: schedule

Most Relevant Blog Posts:

  • Model Storage
  • New Machine Learning Features in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Fairness in Machine Learning

Other Related Blog Posts:

  • A Multivariate Time Series Forecasting Guide
  • Identification of Seasonality in Time Series
  • Global Explanation Capabilities
  • ML Explainability for Classification and Regression
  • ML Explainability for Time Series

entry.title: From Copilot to Colleague: How SAP Is Shaping the Next Era of AI Driven Procurement
entry.content:
From Copilot to Colleague: How SAP Is Shaping the Next Era of AI‑Driven Procurement 🤖

Artificial intelligence has crossed a critical threshold in enterprise software. What began as experimentation with chat interfaces has rapidly evolved into a strategic conversation about how work itself is performed. In a recent discussion with Ryan Skorupski, Senior Director of AI/ML at SAP Ariba, the conversation explored how AI is moving from assistive tooling to embedded intelligence—and what that shift means for procurement leaders navigating increasing complexity, data volume, and speed expectations. 🚀

AI Didn’t Arrive Overnight—But the Moment Has Arrived 🌱

While generative AI may feel new, the technologies behind it—machine learning, language modeling, and predictive analytics—have been advancing in academia and research environments for more than a decade. What changed was accessibility. When large language models became available to a broader audience, leaders experienced firsthand how AI could reason across information, synthesize insights, and support decision‑making at scale.

For procurement, this marked a turning point. AI was no longer limited to narrow automation use cases; it became capable of understanding context, language, and intent. As Ryan noted, this moment forced organizations to rethink not just what they automate, but how procurement work should be designed in an AI‑first world. 💡

Beyond Chatbots: The Rise of the Procurement Copilot ✈️

Much of today’s AI adoption centers on copilots—context‑aware assistants embedded directly into enterprise applications. In SAP Ariba, copilots are designed to work alongside users, helping them navigate sourcing events, analyze data, and interact with complex workflows using natural language.

Unlike traditional chatbots, copilots are grounded in business context. They understand procurement data, permissions, and workflows, allowing them to assist with real tasks—not just answer questions. This represents a significant step forward in usability and productivity, particularly for teams balancing speed, compliance, and accuracy. 🔍

The Next Leap: Agentic AI in Procurement 🤝

While copilots enhance productivity, Ryan highlighted that the next major evolution is AI agents—systems capable of taking action, not just providing guidance. An AI agent can assemble an RFP, analyze historical sourcing events, recommend negotiation strategies, and prepare supplier communications with minimal human intervention.

Importantly, the near‑term goal is not perfection or full autonomy. The value today lies in time savings and scale. AI agents can handle large portions of repetitive, rules‑based work, while humans remain in the loop to review, adjust, and approve outcomes. Over time, as trust and accuracy improve, higher levels of autonomy become possible—especially for low‑risk, high‑volume categories. ⚖️

Why Data Foundations Matter More Than Models 🧠

A recurring theme in the discussion was that AI success depends less on algorithms and more on data quality and structure. Procurement data is often fragmented, inconsistently labeled, or repurposed in ways AI cannot easily interpret. AI cannot “clean” data without understanding its meaning.

This is where SAP’s investment in knowledge graphs becomes critical. By layering semantic meaning over enterprise data—defining what each field, object, and API represents in natural language—AI systems can reason about business data the same way people do. Knowledge graphs provide the connective tissue that allows AI to scale reliably across procurement, finance, and supply chain domains. 📊

Training AI Responsibly—Without Compromising Trust 🔐

One of the most common concerns from customers is how enterprise data is used by AI. Ryan clarified that SAP’s approach centers on pre‑trained models that leverage customer data at inference time, not to train shared public models.

Looking ahead, however, the greatest value will come from customer‑specific AI—models fine‑tuned to an organization’s processes, terminology, and data. This mirrors how employees are trained internally and enables AI to function as a true digital colleague. Strong governance, security, and transparency will remain essential as this capability evolves. 🧩

A New Interaction Model for Enterprise Work 👓

As AI matures, the way users interact with enterprise systems will change. Static dashboards may give way to dynamically generated views. Mobile and tablet‑based approvals will become more common. Over time, augmented and mixed‑reality interfaces may further reduce friction by delivering insights directly in context.

The unifying theme is abstraction: AI handles orchestration, data retrieval, and execution, while users focus on judgment, strategy, and outcomes. This shift has the potential to significantly reshape how procurement teams operate at scale. 🌐

The Future of Procurement Is Human‑Led, AI‑Powered 🎯

AI will not replace procurement professionals—but it will redefine their role. The future belongs to organizations that invest early in data foundations, AI literacy, and trust‑based adoption. As AI evolves from tool, to copilot, to agent, SAP’s focus remains clear: embed intelligence responsibly, keep humans in control, and deliver tangible business value.

The question is no longer whether AI will transform procurement—but how intentionally leaders choose to shape that transformation. 🔮

LISTEN HERE:

Apple:  https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/episode-029-diving-deep-into-ai-in-procurement-with/id1764078646?i=1000760634770

Spotify:  Spotify:  https://open.spotify.com/episode/0DoASvDgt3tzox6D6KAmu6?si=QXxta4KzS6mO7YF2SS9MVQ

entry.title: The Rise of Agentic AI: Transforming Enterprise Intelligence in SAP Ecosystems
entry.content:
Introduction

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is fundamentally reshaping how modern enterprises operate, innovate, and compete. What began as simple automation has now evolved into intelligent systems capable of learning, adapting, and making decisions.

Today, we are entering a transformative new phase the era of Agentic AI where AI systems no longer just respond to inputs but actively plan, reason, act, and collaborate to achieve complex business goals.

I remember when AI was mostly about crunching numbers for forecasts. Now, with SAP innovation,  it's stepping into full-blown independence. Companies using SAP S/4HANA stand out because they can weave these agents right into their daily grind, turning rigid processes into something flexible and smart

For SAP-driven organizations, this shift represents not just a technological upgrade, but a paradigm change in how business processes are executed, optimized, and orchestrated.

From Task Automation to Agentic Systems

The evolution of AI reflects a steady progression toward greater autonomy and intelligence. Each phase has contributed to building the foundation for today’s agent-driven ecosystems.

1. Rule-Based Systems: The Foundation of Automation

Early AI systems were deterministic and logic-driven. These rule-based systems relied on predefined conditions and workflows to execute tasks.

Examples included:

  • Basic chatbots
  • Workflow automation engines
  • Decision trees in ERP systems

While effective for structured tasks, these systems lacked adaptability and could not handle ambiguity or dynamic environments common in enterprise operations.

2. Machine Learning & Neural Networks: Learning from Data

The 1990s marked a significant shift toward data-driven intelligence with the rise of Machine Learning (ML) and Neural Networks.

That was a shift. Systems started pulling lessons from piles of data. Think IBM Watson showing off its thinking chops, or AlexNet flipping image analysis upside down with deep layers. What that meant was better guesses over time - spotting demand trends, sniffing out risks, keeping quality in check. In SAP, it popped up in supply chain modules or finance dashboards, making predictions feel less like guesswork.

Key breakthroughs included systems like:

  • IBM Watson, which demonstrated cognitive computing capabilities
  • AlexNet, which revolutionized image recognition through deep learning

These innovations enabled systems to:

  • Learn patterns from large datasets
  • Improve predictions over time
  • Support decision-making in areas like demand forecasting, risk analysis, and quality management

In SAP environments, this phase enabled predictive analytics and embedded intelligence within modules such as supply chain planning and finance.

3. Deep Learning, Generative AI & NLP: Human-Like Intelligence

Recent advancements in Deep Learning, Natural Language Processing (NLP), and Generative AI have accelerated AI adoption across enterprises.

Deep learning took it further, especially with natural language and generative tools. Large models now chat like people, whip up reports, or handle queries in apps. For SAP users, that translates to copilots that guide you through tasks or interfaces where you just talk it out. Insights come automatically, no digging required. Pretty handy when you're buried in data.

Large Language Models (LLMs) now enable systems to:

  • Understand and generate human-like language
  • Automate documentation and reporting
  • Provide conversational interfaces for enterprise applications

This has significantly enhanced user experience in SAP ecosystems through:

  • Intelligent copilots
  • Conversational AI interfaces
  • Automated insights generation

The Frontier: Agentic AI

But here's the exciting bit , agentic AI pulls all that together into something proactive. These agents don't wait for orders. They chase goals, weigh options in changing situations, loop in other agents or folks, and tweak as they go. No more hostile into narrow lanes. Instead, they orchestrate across tools, data, everything.

All these advancements converge into the evolution: Agentic AI.

 

Agentic AI systems are characterized by their ability to:

  • Act autonomously toward defined goals
  • Reason and make decisions in dynamic environments
  • Collaborate with other agents and humans
  • Continuously learn and adapt

Unlike traditional AI, which operates within predefined boundaries, Agentic AI introduces goal-driven orchestration across systems, processes, and data.

Why Agentic AI Matters for SAP Enterprises

In the context of SAP S/4HANA and intelligent enterprise frameworks, Agentic AI unlocks new capabilities:

  • End-to-End Process Automation
    AI agents can manage complete workflows from demand sensing to order fulfillment without constant human intervention.
  • Intelligent Decision-Making
    Agents can evaluate multiple scenarios in real time, enabling proactive and optimized business decisions.
  • Cross-Functional Collaboration
    AI agents can interact across SAP modules (MM, SD, TM, Finance), breaking silos and improving process integration.
  • Adaptive Supply Chains
    Agentic systems can dynamically respond to disruptions, demand fluctuations, and logistics constraints. From what I've seen in SAP environments, this hits hard on a few fronts. End-to-end automation stands out. An agent might sense demand, route orders, and wrap fulfillment all on its own, cutting out the handoffs. Real issue tends to be human bottlenecks - this sidesteps them.

    Decision-making gets sharper too. Picture evaluating scenarios live, picking the best path without delay. Proactive moves like that optimize on the spot. And collaboration? Agents bridge modules - materials management, sales, transport, finance - smashing those old silos. Processes flow smoother because of it Supply chains adapt faster with this. Disruptions hit, demands swing, logistics snag - agents adjust dynamically. No more reactive scrambling. It's what actually happens in practice for businesses leaning on SAP. They build resilient setups that bend instead of break.

Conclusion

The journey from rule-based automation to Agentic AI represents a fundamental shift in enterprise intelligence. Organizations leveraging SAP platforms are uniquely positioned to harness this transformation by embedding AI agents into their core processes.

The way I see it, this evolution from basic rules to full agents marks a deep change. SAP outfits are primed for it, embedding these smarts into core ops. Thing is, as we push toward self-running systems, agentic AI drives the edge - efficiency ramps up, agility sharpens, and staying ahead gets easier in a fast digital world. Turns out, the ones who grab it first will pull away.

As businesses move toward autonomous, intelligent ecosystems, Agentic AI will become the cornerstone of innovation driving efficiency, agility, and competitive advantage in the digital era.

entry.title: Unlock the Power of Artificial Intelligence with SAP Learning: Courses, Certifications & More
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming the way businesses operate, making it crucial to stay up-to-date with the latest advancements and best practices. SAP Learning is at the forefront of this revolution, offering a comprehensive suite of training opportunities tailored to boost your expertise and career in AI.

Featured Learning on SAP: Joule

Meet Joule—SAP’s intelligent AI assistant designed to accelerate business transformation. Whether you’re new to Joule or aiming to integrate AI-driven agents throughout your organizational system landscape, SAP’s curated courses will help you harness its full potential:

  • Introducing Joule: Get started with the basics and discover how Joule is reshaping intelligent business processes.
  • Boosting AI-driven Business Transformation with Joule Agents: Learn to deploy AI agents that unlock new efficiencies and innovation.
  • Implementing Joule Across Your Organizational System Landscape: Dive into a guided journey for seamless integration across your enterprise.

AI Foundation: Build, Solve, and Certify

Ready to put generative AI to work? SAP offers hands-on training and globally recognized certifications:

  • Solve Your Business Problems Using Prompts and LLMs in SAP Generative AI Hub: Leverage large language models (LLMs) and intelligent prompts to address real-world challenges.
  • SAP Certified – SAP Generative AI Developer: Validate your expertise and showcase your skill set with this in-demand certification, tailored for today’s data-driven environment.

Explore More: Expert-Led Sessions & Learning Journeys

SAP Learning goes beyond traditional courses. Benefit from expert-led live sessions and immersive, guided learning paths that ensure you not only gain knowledge but also practical, real-world skills. Our Artificial Intelligence Featured Learning Page is your one-stop shop to discover the full spectrum of AI offerings.

Why Choose SAP Learning for AI?

  • Skill-building for the future: Acquire knowledge that aligns with the latest business needs and technological breakthroughs.
  • Expert guidance: Learn from top professionals and AI practitioners.
  • Certification: Stand out with credentials that demonstrate your AI expertise to employers and peers.

Take the next step in your AI journey with SAP Learning.
Master artificial intelligence, get certified, and shape the future of business innovation. Visit the Artificial Intelligence Featured Learning Page to get started today!

entry.title: Why data fragmentation limits AI in SAP landscapes
entry.content:
Almost every conversation these days related to SAP contains some reference to AI. This doesn't surprise me, as I don't know any person today that it's not acquainted with AI somehow. Even those whose knowledge on technology is very little have become accostumed to it.

When it comes to enterprise technology, AI has already proven capabilities, along with the validation of real-world implementations. Models being created today can predict outcomes, automate processes, and produce insights like never before — we personally have performed multiple trials in a controlled environment that provided us with very successful outcomes. However, the problems begin to occur when you step outside of these controlled environments and try to integrate AI into how businesses truly function, because generally speaking, there is an issue unrelated to the actual model. I think things would be easier if the following issue was understood: almost all problems that come with the use of AI, even in enterprise technology, are entirely human.

The most prevalent issue we meet is with the current state of the data on which those models are built. The SAP landscape has not been built in a straightforward, linear manner. SAP environments develop over time, sometimes decades, and they accumulate all types of complexity from different systems, customizations, and integrations. What you actually find when trying to integrate AI into a business is not one version of a “true” set of data; it’s numerous versions of that same set of data existing simultaneously. This is where the difficulty in the implementation of AI begins.

From our observations, typically when implemented in a targeted manner, AI can quickly demonstrate its intended utility. You understand what it is you want to do, prepare your data and create your model, then produce relevant results. That’s not the problem. The contingency occurs immediately after implementation when there is momentum to take that across the enterprise as part of normal operations. At the time of enterprise rollout, the model is no longer operating against a pre-qualified dataset, but is functioning against the full complexity of the SAP ecosystem. Data is sourced from multiple places, using different formats, at different frequencies and sometimes even with differing definitions of the same element. The model continues to perform in its intended manner, but at this point, it is performing on a fragmented foundation.

This scenario was demonstrated to us on a recent project in the energy market where our objective was to forecast energy demand and supply as well as price by aggregating historical production and operational data with meteorological variables. The end product was a neural network model that has been trained with 3+ years of historical data, and the results will be made available through a dashboard to provide users with expected outcomes in order to support their decision-making process.

When viewed through the lens of modeling, everything operated as expected. The model predictions aligned with the actual results and provided analytical evidence to support previously undiscovered trends. However, the challenge was not necessarily the model itself, but rather the data that it was being provided either directly or indirectly to derive these insights.

We aggregated a multitude of data sources including electricity data, weather patterns, and pricing data to make predictions and develop forecasts. Each data source had its own unique structure, timing and granularity. Therefore, there were occasions where small misalignment between these disparate data sources would result in inconsistent predicted results.

This is where the most significant issues began to emerge, as various teams were relying on a different set of datasets when the model generated a forecast, which led to a disconnect from one team to the next. As such, teams would validate the model output against their own internal generated data, which would then lead to the model output being challenged instead of acted upon. While the insights were available, confidence in the insights was not. We’ve seen this pattern countless times, where fragmented data doesn’t stop AI from generating insights, but it does stop people from trusting and acting on them.

What we've learned is that scaling AI in SAP environments is actually a lot more about fixing the underlying data than about building a better AI model. Once you get your data to be more consistent and integrated and shared more easily across systems, everything else becomes a lot easier to do and move along at a faster pace.

We have experienced firsthand how a technically safe solution can have trouble because of data alignment issues, and we have seen how fairly simple models can actually generate significant value as long as your data foundation is solid. So the difference between these two situations is not the AI itself, but rather the environment in which the AI is being used.

Ultimately, the only time AI will matter is when it can lead to actual business decisions that take place. And in SAP landscapes, the transition from insight to action is very much dependent upon the connectivity and reliability of the data.

Therefore, while it may seem logical to focus on what your model can do for you, the more critical question for businesses is whether their data is ready to support scaling of AI. If it's not, scaling AI will always feel harder than it should be, no matter how good your models are.

entry.title: Text Processing in NLP
entry.content:
Introduction:

NLP is a field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that allows computers to understand, interpret, and utilize human language in context. The reason human language is so complicated is primarily due to grammar, emotions, context words and terminologies of a particular field as well as different writing styles. The rise of Natural language processing:NLP enables machines to dissect this language and transmute unstructured text into structured data, which is easily consumable by computers.

Body:

Natural Language Processing employs a number of textual preprocessing methodologies including tokenization, lemmatization, vectorization and similarity modeling. These techniques are used to split the text into smaller pieces (such as words or sentences), extract root words, convert that text into a numerical vector and compare meaning between two sentences/sentences/documents. Further, after the data is processed it can feed machine learning and deep learning models for intelligent decision making and predictions.

Tokenization :

Tokenization breaks down a given sentence or text into smaller parts called tokens. Computers cannot read human sentence as a whole. Hence, first NLP divides the text into smaller chunks for processing.

Example:

Input: “NLP is very interesting”

Output : ["NLP", "is", "very", "interesting"]

Lemmatization

Lemmatization is the process of converting a word to its base or root form known as a lemma. This allows NLP systems to comprehend that different variants of a term signify the same thing.

Input :["running", "studies", "better"]

Output : ["run", "study", "good"]

Vectorization

Vectorization is the process of converting text or words into numerical form (vectors) so that machine learning models can understand and process them.TF-IDF Vectorization is an NLP technique used to convert text into numerical vectors based on the importance of words in a document. TF-IDF helps identify which words are important and meaningful in a sentence or document

Input

["I love NLP", "NLP is interesting"]

Output : 

[

 [1, 1, 1, 0],

 [0, 1, 0, 1]

]

Find similarity:

In NLP, finding similarity is the task of two texts to see how similar they are.It teaches computers to understand when two sentences are saying the same thing.Word Embeddings, Cosine Similarity and TF-IDF are commonly used techniques. Typically, the similarity score is between 0 and 1.

Input

Sentence 1: "I love NLP"

Sentence 2: "I like NLP"

 

Output

0.82

 

Summary: 

NLP is widely used these days in chatbots, virtual assistants, search engines, translation systems, sentiment analysis, recommendation systems, spam filtering, and AI-powered enterprise applications. Popular applications include Siri, Alexa, Google Translate, ChatGPT and customer service bots. NLP is a key factor in enhancing human-computer interaction and automating language-based tasks in different sectors.

entry.title: Building a Business-Critical Readiness Score for SAP S/4HANA Transformations
entry.content:
Building a Business-Critical Readiness Score for SAP S/4HANA Transformations

In many SAP S/4HANA programs, teams spend a lot of time tracking tasks, milestones, and project plans. On paper, everything may look fine. But when cutover gets closer, the real question is not whether the work is technically “done.” The real question is whether the business is actually ready.

That is where a readiness score can help.

A readiness score gives transformation teams a practical way to measure how prepared a program really is. Instead of relying only on status updates or gut feel, it creates a simple way to look at the health of the transformation across business, data, integration, testing, and cutover. In my experience, this kind of view is often what separates a smooth go-live from a painful one.

SAP S/4HANA Transformation Readiness Dashboard

Illustrative example of a transformation readiness dashboard showing key readiness indicators across business, data, testing, integration, and cutover domains.

Why project progress is not the same as readiness

One of the biggest mistakes in large SAP programs is assuming that progress automatically means readiness. A team may have completed design workshops, built the technical solution, and even started testing, but still be far from ready to go live. The reason is simple: transformation success depends on much more than completed tasks.

For example, the project may look healthy from a schedule perspective, but master data could still be inconsistent. Interfaces may work in the development environment but fail under real business volumes. Business users may not yet trust the new process. Cutover activities may still depend on too many manual steps. None of these issues always show up clearly in a standard project dashboard.

A readiness score helps expose those gaps early. It creates a more honest picture of whether the organization can actually move into production with confidence.

What a readiness score should cover

A useful readiness score should reflect the full transformation picture, not just one part of it. If it only focuses on testing, then data issues and organizational concerns may get missed. If it only focuses on data, then integration and cutover risks may be underestimated. The score needs to bring all the important pieces together.

The main areas I would include are:

  • Business process fit.

  • Master data quality.

  • Integration stability.

  • Test completeness.

  • Cutover preparedness.

  • User and organizational readiness.

  • Support and hypercare readiness.

Illustrative readiness scorecard used to evaluate critical transformation domains before go-live decisions.

Each of these areas matters because a weakness in any one of them can create serious problems at go-live. A program is only as strong as its weakest readiness area.

A simple way to score it

The best readiness models are usually the easiest to understand. I prefer a simple scoring scale, such as 0 to 5 for each domain, with weights based on business importance. Then the total can be converted into a 100-point readiness index that leadership can interpret quickly.

For example:

 
Area What it tells you
Business process fitWhether the future process truly supports how the business works.
Master data qualityWhether the data is clean and ready for migration.
Integration stabilityWhether interfaces and messages are behaving reliably.
Test completenessWhether the key business scenarios have been validated.
Cutover preparednessWhether the move to production can happen with control.
User readinessWhether people are prepared for the new process.
Support readinessWhether the team can handle issues after go-live.

Once this is scored, the program can use a simple interpretation such as:

  • Above 85: strong readiness.

  • 70 to 85: moderate risk, but manageable with active mitigation.

  • Below 70: significant concern and likely not ready for go-live.

The exact numbers can vary, but the point is to make readiness visible and easy to act on.

What this looks like in real life

Let’s say a transformation program is approaching cutover. The team feels confident because testing is mostly complete and the technical build is done. But once the readiness score is updated, a different picture appears. Data quality is still weak, a few critical interfaces have unstable behavior, and the business has not fully signed off on the future process.

That score changes the conversation.

Instead of asking, “Are we on track?”, leadership starts asking, “What do we need to fix before we can safely go live?” That shift is important. It moves the program from activity tracking to risk management.

In a complex SAP environment, that is often the difference between success and rework.

Why AI can improve the score

AI can make readiness scoring even more useful by turning it from a static checklist into a predictive tool. Instead of waiting for issues to become visible in late testing or cutover rehearsal, AI can help identify patterns earlier.

Illustrative example showing how AI-driven insights can identify emerging risks and improve transformation readiness visibility.

For example, repeated migration errors may signal data quality issues. A spike in defects in one process area may indicate that testing coverage is incomplete. Frequent manual workarounds during cutover simulation may show that the go-live plan is too fragile. These are all signals that can be captured and used to improve the readiness score.

This is where the combination of human judgment and predictive insight becomes powerful. The business provides context, and the data provides early warning.

Why governance matters

A readiness score only works if people actually use it. It should not be treated as a one-time presentation slide. It needs to become part of the program’s regular governance rhythm.

Illustrative governance framework showing how readiness scoring can support operational, program, and executive decision-making.

A good approach is to review it at three levels:

  • Workstream level, where owners update their areas honestly and regularly.

  • Program level, where trends and risks are discussed across teams.

  • Executive level, where the score supports go/no-go decisions.

The most important thing is consistency. If each team scores itself differently, the model loses value. But if everyone uses the same rules and the same definitions, the score becomes a trusted decision tool.

Common mistakes to avoid

There are a few traps I have seen in transformation programs.

The first is making the score too complicated. If it takes too long to update or explain, people stop trusting it. The score should be simple enough to maintain but strong enough to reflect real risk.

The second is measuring activity instead of readiness. Completing a task is not the same as being ready. A test case closed does not mean the business process works. A migration file loaded does not mean the data is correct. Leadership should always look for evidence of readiness, not just evidence of effort.

The third is leaving the business out of the conversation. SAP transformation is not only a technical exercise. If the business does not validate the future process, if users are not prepared, or if support ownership is unclear, the program is still exposed.

A practical takeaway

If you are leading an SAP S/4HANA transformation, I would strongly recommend building a readiness score early in the program. It does not need to be perfect. It just needs to be honest, consistent, and useful.

A simple scorecard can prevent a lot of avoidable trouble. It helps leadership see risks before they become incidents. It helps teams focus on what matters most. And it gives the entire program a clearer picture of whether it is truly ready for the next step.

In large transformations, that kind of clarity is valuable. Sometimes the difference between a successful go-live and a difficult one is not effort — it is visibility.

entry.title: Hands-on Tutorial: Machine Learning with SAP HANA Cloud and BAS
entry.content:
Machine Learning on structured data might not be what many people have in mind when talking about AI. Yet, it is a form of AI and the massive interest in Large Language Models, agents and assistants is also leading to many new Machine Learning Projects. 

With this blog you can get hands-on with Machine Learning by implementing a time-series forecast in SAP HANA Cloud. In the requirements and projects that I am seeing, time-series forecasting is by far the most popular Machine Learning approach in a SAP related project. From Accounts Receivable forecasting, to demand forecasting and even solar power prediction, a time-series forecast might get you there.

IMPORTANT: You can download all code from this repository.

 

Architecture

The architecture for this example is as straightforward as it can be. Your data is in SAP HANA Cloud, where we also have Machine Learning algorithms (Predictive Analysis Library and Automated Predictive Library). We then use Python in a Jupyter Notebook running in SAP Business Application Studio to trigger the Machine Learning algorithms in SAP HANA Cloud. This is possible with SAP's hana_ml Python package. You can also choose any other Python environment of your choice to use that package to trigger the built-in algorithms. No data ever has to leave SAP HANA Cloud, since data and ML algorithm are already in the same place.

This blog focuses mostly on a stand-alone SAP HANA Cloud. However, you can also trigger the same Machine Learning in SAP Datasphere's embedded SAP HANA Cloud. This blog has more details. 

More components are typically needed for a productive environment. To automate the creation of predictions the Python code can be schedules for instance with Cloud Foundry or SAP AI Core. And the time-series algorithm we are using is just one of 100+ algorithms for you to choose from. Here is an overview. See the hana_ml documentation for details.

 

Prerequisites

This blog assumes that:

  • You have SAP Business Application Studio up and running and configured as described in "Setting up Python in SAP Business Application Studio to trigger HANA Cloud Machine Learning"
  • You have access to a SAP HANA Cloud instance, that is configured as described in the same above blog.

 

Data Load

You will use historic data from the Swiss tourism area to predict how many nights people will spend in a hotel each month in the next 12 months. Download the file HOTELNIGHTS.csv and import it into SAP HANA Cloud, for instance with the SAP HANA Database Explorer. Select "Import Data".

 

Click through the options:

  • name the new table: HOTELNIGHTS
  • set the following column data types
    • MONTH: Date
    • ARRIVALS: Integer
    • HOTELNIGHTS: Integer

 

You will see that the information in that table is quite detailed. It shows for the period from January 2020 to March 2026 by month how many people from which Country spent how many nights in a hotel in the various regions of Switzerland.

 

Time-series forecast

Now use the data for a time-series forecast! Script in Python and the hana_ml package translates your logic into SQL and has SAP HANA Cloud do the work. 

IMPORTANT: You can download the notebook HANAML time series forecast.ipynb, which has the following code included. If you upload that file into your environment and execute the first cell, you will be prompted to select the Python environment. This can be easily overlooked...

If you are meeting the prerequisites (see above), you should already be able to connect form a Jupyter notebook in BAS to connect to your SAP HANA Cloud. Continue now by storing the database user's details a in a credentials file.

{
  "SAP_HANA_CLOUD": {
    "HANA_ADDRESS": "YOURENDPOINT",
    "HANA_PORT": 443,
    "HANA_USER": "AIUSER",
    "HANA_PASSWORD": "YOURSECRETPASSWORD"
  }
}

 

Load these credentials in Python and verify that the connection still returns True.

import json
with open('./credentials.json', 'r') as creds:
  credentials = json.load(creds)
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_ADDRESS  = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_ADDRESS"]
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PORT     = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_PORT"]
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_USER     = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_USER"]
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PASSWORD = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_PASSWORD"]
import hana_ml.dataframe as dataframe
conn = dataframe.ConnectionContext(address  = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_ADDRESS,
                                   port     = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PORT,
                                   user     = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_USER,
                                   password = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PASSWORD)
conn.connection.isconnected()

In case you see a compatibility warning for Python 3.0.2, feel free to ignore it.

Continue by creating a hana_ml DataFrame, which points to the Data in SAP HANA Cloud. The data remains in SAP HANA Cloud. No records have yet left the system. This is by default and on purpose. The intention is to keep the data in the system and do any data processing within SAP HANA Cloud.

data_hdf = conn.table('HOTELNIGHTS')

 

It is possible to retrieve data though. You just need to request it with the collect()-method, which downloads the data of the hana_ml DataFrame as Pandas DataFrame. To avoid downloading all records, first restrict the data to 5 rows with the head()-method. The sequence is very important here. First reduce the number of records, then download.

data_hdf.head(5).collect()

 

To get a more detailed feel for the data, have some statistics calculated.

data_hdf.describe().collect()

 

There are 13 Swiss regions in the data for example and the maximum number of hotel nights in a specific months in a specific region from a specific country is 223091. Important here is the fact that every value in that table was calculated in SAP HANA Cloud.
