Imagine you are working with a massive table, you will want to leverage the processing power of SAP HANA Cloud. That's ensured here, since the hana_ml package created the necessary SQL statement. That SQL syntax can be seen by adding ".select_statement" to the command. The screenshot shows only a fraction of the SQL.

import pprint
pprint.pp(data_hdf.describe().select_statement)

 

One of many other ways to explore the data is an interactive standard report.

from hana_ml.visualizers.unified_report import UnifiedReport
UnifiedReport(data_hdf).build().display()

 

We are happy with the data and need to prepare it now for a monthly forecast of all hotel nights in the country. Aggregate by month. 

agg_hdf = data_hdf.agg([('sum', 'HOTELNIGHTS', 'HOTELNIGHTS')], group_by='MONTH')
agg_hdf = agg_hdf.sort('MONTH')
agg_hdf.head(5).collect()

As usual, that aggregation is also done on SAP HANA Cloud and only 5 aggregated values are downloaded. Also as usual, you could add ".select_statement" to see the exact SQL syntax that is used.

Plot the monthly data to get a visual impression for the data. This requires the installation of another Python package. Run this cell once. After the package is installed:

  • you may want to delete the cell (or comment it out) to avoid unnecessary reinstallation attempts)
  • and you may need to restart the Python kernel so that the new package can be used. Click the "Restart" icon in the menu on top and run the earlier cells again.
!pip install nbformat # Once installed, turn this line into a comment to avoid running it again

 

Download the monthly aggregates and create a line chart.

import plotly.express as px
data_df = agg_hdf.collect()
fig =  px.line(data_df, x='MONTH', y='HOTELNIGHTS', title='Hotel nights by Month')
fig.show()

There is a beautiful repeating pattern in the data. True seasonality. Peaks in the summer, lows in November and lower peaks in winter. Train a time-series model! We use the AdditiveModelForecast which is an implementation of Meta's Prophet algorithm.

from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.tsa.additive_model_forecast import AdditiveModelForecast
amf = AdditiveModelForecast()
amf.fit(data=agg_hdf)

 

Explore the trained model in another interactive standard report.

from hana_ml.visualizers.unified_report import UnifiedReport
UnifiedReport(amf).build().display()

 

With that trained model we can forecast the unknown future. Just prepare a hana_ml DataFrame that contains the months (or dates), for which we want forecasts. In a productive scenario you will probably connect to a table that's permanently updated with new records. Hence, to keep the code dynamic for scheduling, find out from the data the most recent month for which data is available. 

str_lastdate = agg_hdf.tail(1, ref_col='MONTH').collect().iloc[0,0]
str_lastdate = str(str_lastdate)[0:10]
print(str_lastdate)

 

Use that month as basis create the required dates for the forecast. We need 12 records, always the first day of the following 12 months.

from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.random import binomial
months_to_forecast=12
future_hdf = binomial(conn, n=1, p=1, num_random=months_to_forecast)
future_hdf = future_hdf.select('*', (f'''ADD_MONTHS(TO_DATE ('{str_lastdate}', 'YYYY-MM-DD'), ID+1)''', 'MONTH') )
future_hdf = future_hdf.select('MONTH', ('0', 'TARGET'))
future_hdf.head(20).collect()

 

Apply the trained model to predict those dates. And you get the prediction (YHAT) as well as the prediction interval (YHAT_LOWER and YHAT_UPPER).

predicted_hdf = amf.predict(data=future_hdf)
predicted_hdf.head(20).collect()

Before using the predictions, have a closer look at the trained model and the forecasted values in the standard report.

 

The outlier tab points out a few months in 2020 that are unusual. Indeed, there was something that year that impacted travelling and hotel nights....  Cutting off that old history would be a good idea. You could go back to the top of the Notebook and add a filter to the data, ie 

data_hdf = data_hdf.filter("YEAR(MONTH) >= 2022")

 

In this tutorial we continue with the model as it is. You could save the predictions just as they are. However, you may also want to combine the past actuals with the predictions into a single table since that might make it easier to put a dashboard on top for instance.

predicted_hdf = predicted_hdf.select('MONTH',
                                    ('NULL', 'HOTELNIGHTS'),
                                    ('YHAT', 'FORECAST'),
                                    ('YHAT_LOWER', 'FORECAST_LOWER'),
                                    ('YHAT_UPPER', 'FORECAST_UPPER'))
agg_hdf = agg_hdf.select('*', 
                        ('NULL', 'FORECAST'),
                        ('NULL', 'FORECAST_LOWER'),
                        ('NULL', 'FORECAST_UPPER'))
actualsandpred_hdf = predicted_hdf.union(agg_hdf)
actualsandpred_hdf.sort('MONTH').tail(5).collect()

 

Finally, save that combined data into a table in SAP HANA Cloud. In case that table doesn't exist yet, it will be automatically created in the format of the hana_ml DataFrame.

actualsandpred_hdf.save('HOTELNIGHTS_FORECAST', force=True)

 

With this you have enriched your data in SAP HANA Cloud through Machine Learning! The actuals in the HOTELNIGHTS column end in March 2026 with the FORECAST continuing on from April 2026 to March 2027.

 

Related topics

With such a forecast you can already achieve a lot. You may also want to extend it further or use a completely different algorithm. These resources can help you along, but feel free to ask if you have any questions!

  • Documentation hana_ml package
  • Documentation of the underlying Predictive Analysis Library
  • Sample code for the Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) and also the Automated Predictive Library (APL)

Or if you want to have the code written for you, check out this "Hands-on Tutorial: Vibe coding SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning with BAS, Python, Claude and Cline".

entry.title: Hands-on Tutorial: Vibe coding SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning with BAS, Python, Claude and Cline
entry.content:
Have a Large Language Model write the Machine Learning code for you next project!

Sit back and watch the SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) use Anthropic Claude to create Jupyter Notebooks and write the Python code to trigger the Predictive Analysis Library in SAP HANA Cloud (or SAP Datasphere). This example uses Claude, but there is a long list of models to choose from through SAP AI Core. In this tutorial we go through a time-series forecast, but that's just an example, you can use this ability to vibe code in BAS for all sorts of requirements.

It's fantastic to see how the code is written automatically, and how good the code is! But to keep it real, work with that automatically created code as if it had been written by a human intern. Inspect the code to ensure it does what you want it to do.

  • Architecture
  • Prerequisites
  • Setup
  • Vibe coding test
  • Adding the latest documentation
  • Vibe code HANA Machine Learning
  • Summary

Big thanks go to @sherwin_emami for introducing me to Cline and for showing me the ropes of vibe coding in BAS!

 

Architecture

The architecture centers around the SAP Business Application Studio, where you can already do you Python scripting in a Jupyter Notebook by hand. 

The coding agent Cline is installed as extension in BAS. Cline connects to SAP AI Core to integrate the Large Language Models (LLM) that process the user requests and produce the code. You can choose which LLM you want to use. In this example we use Claude, which is hosted outside the SAP environment. Other models, i.e. from Mistral, are hosted on SAP infrastructure.

SAP HANA Cloud holds the data and Machine Learning algorithms. The same setup would also work with the SAP HANA Cloud that is embedded in SAP Datasphere.

Optionally, you can also add the Context7 MCP server to Cline to get access to additional / current software documentation.

You may have heard of the SAP Business AI Platform, which is also shown in the architecture diagram. However, here we use the same components that you know from the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). You can implement this scenario on any BTP environment.

 

Prerequisites

You will need the following components to implement this blog's scenario:

  • SAP Business Application Studio, configured to run Jupyter Notebooks, see this blog
  • SAP HANA Cloud
    • configured to run the Predictive Analysis Library, see the same blog
    • with the table HOTELNIGHTS from this other blog uploaded
  • SAP AI Core, on an Extended Service Plan. SAP AI Launchpad is not needed for the vibe coding.

 

Setup

Install the Cline extension into the BAS. This should take only a few seconds.

 

Then get a Service Key from your SAP AI Core instance.

 

Continue in the Cline extension and give it access to SAP AI Core.

  • Select the option "Bring my own API key".
  • From the "API Provider" dropdown select "SAP AI Core" and enter the values from your SAP AI Core Service Key:
    • AI Core Client ID: value of clientid
    • AI Core Client Secret: value of clientsecret
    • AI Core Base URL: value of AI_API_URL
    • AI Core Auth URL: value of url
    • AI Core Resource Group: By default the Resource Group is "default", unless you created your own in SAP AI Core
  • Tick "Orchestration Mode".  This makes all applicable models from SAP AI Core available, without having to deploy them first
  • Select your preferred model. Here I am going with Claude 4.7 Opus.

 

Click "Continue" and Cline is asking what it can do your you 

 

Vibe coding test

Use a simple task to get familiar with Cline. It's important to know the distinction between "Plan" and "Act" mode. 

Plan mode: Typically it is best to start in Plan mode. You describe what you would Cline built for you and Cline is building a plan for achieving this. This can be a discussion, which shapes how to address this task. In this phase, Cline can access files (if you grant access), but it cannot make any changes to your existing file or create new files.

Act mode: Once you are happy with the plan you can switch to the "Act" mode, in which Cline executes the plan. Here Cline can, for instance, create new files or modify existing ones.

Let's try it out!

First select the folder in which you want to work, ie "home/user/projects/hanaml". If you are unsure about the folder, see this blog.

Make sure that Cline is in "Plan" mode (the little button below the prompt window). Check which permissions you want to give Cline. Then enter this task and Cline is creating the plan to achieve this.

Create a jupyter notebook that contains a greeting

 

We are happy with the plan and want this executed. Click on the "Act" tab. You will see how Cline is going through the plan. If you are using the default permissions, you will be asked to confirm the creation of the Notebook file and indeed the file opens up!

 

You can run the code in the notebook. And the file is there as requested. 

 

Mission accomplished, the vibe coding test was successful!

 

A few hints in case this didn't work for you:

  • With Cline's default settings you will probably get a few approval requests. You need to confirm these for the process to continue.
  • These requests are not always immediately shown on screen. Check whether you need to scroll further down in the Cline window to see the latest output.
  • Sometimes Cline seems to hang. Refreshing the browser seems to fix it for me
  • Sometimes Cline opening a file results in the error "The editor could not be opened due to an unexpected error. Please consult the log for more details.". Clicking the "Try again" usually fixes it for me.
  • You know that the "Act" mode has completed, when you see "Start New Task".

 

Adding the latest documentation

Before creating code for your business, let's make Cline even more useful. Currently the Large Language Model that we are using might not know the latest documentation of the libraries that we want to use, particularly the Python package hana_ml.

A popular approach for bringing in the latest documentation is by adding Context7 to the project. There may be alternatives, but Context7 worked well for me. It can be added as MCP server both as local installation and through a remote URL. The local installation through the Cline interface didn't succeed for me, but the remote version worked very well. Funnily enough, you can just ask Cline to add the remote version itself.

Go into the "Plan" mode and request:

Add an MCP server for Context7, use the remote URL

When happy with the plan, switch to the "Act" mode. Confirm the requests if you agree. And the MCP server for Context7 should have been added.

 

Verify this in the Cline settings. In the "MCP Servers" section under "Configure" the new MCP Server should show up. Also, below the prompt window you can see that the new MCP server is enabled and ready for use.

 

Vibe code HANA Machine Learning

Everything we need has been set up. Let's do something useful with this it. This blog has a step-by-step explanation for writing the code to carry out a time-series forecast in SAP HANA Cloud. Hopefully that tutorial is useful to learn how the Machine Learning works, how Python code can instruct the Predictive Analysis Library to create the forecasts, without having to extract the data. Let's put vibe coding to the test, whether it can create such code for us.

First ensure that you have the credentials.json file in your folder, as shown in the same blog.

Then ask your code assistant in "Plan" mode to create the code.

Create a new notebook called “HANA ML Time-series forecast”.  Use the details from @/credentials.json to and the hana_ml package to connect to SAP HANA Cloud. Connect to the table HOTELNIGHTS. Do not download the data. Use hana_ml to have HANA Cloud aggregate this table on the MONTH column, summarising the column HOTELNIGHTS. Use the hana_ml Package to train an AdditiveModelForecast model and predict the next 6 months. Join the actuals and the prediction in a single structure and save as table HOTELNIGHTS_FORECAST_BAS. Test the code and fix any errors. Use context7.

 

It is coming back with a very detailed, pretty looking plan.

 

Click on "Act" to have it executed. Watch how Cline is going through that plan: writing, testing and potentially fixing the code. Pay attention to any Request prompts that might come up at the bottom left. Approve these if you agree. After a few minutes you should have a Notebook!

 

In the new Notebook, first choose your Python kernel on the top right. Then hit "Run All" to test the logic. It's looking good! But not perfect... The dates for the future months, that are predicted, are not the first day of that month.

 

Back to the prompt , and request a change. Here I am skipping the "Plan" mode and request this directly in the "Act" mode.

Change the code so that the forecasted dates are always the first day of the month.

 

And indeed, now the months are correctly dated! The LLMs came up with many different way to create the DataFrame with the dates that need to be predicted. You can see a few in the comments.

 

As requested, the prediction is also saved to SAP HANA Cloud.

 

Summary

It's impressive how efficient and helpful vibe coding can be! 

It can be a massive time-saver, but do remember to verify the code...

entry.title: Multi-Agent LLM Pipeline with SAP RPT and other SAP AI Core LLMs for World Cup 2026 Match Prediction
entry.content:
Football has been a personal obsession since 2002, when a seven-year-old version of me watched Brazil lift the World Cup and decided this sport was basically magic. Twenty-four years later, the obsession hasn't changed but my toolbox has. A few months ago I started working with SAP's Relational Pretrained Transformer (RPT) on enterprise prediction problems and one question wouldn't leave me alone: could this thing predict football?
Spoiler
Spoiler: it can, within the limits of honest machine learning. This post walks through what I built, what I learned, and why one of the most useful things the system does is admit when it doesn't know.
The Problem With Football Predictions (and Enterprise Data)
 
Traditional football prediction has the same shape as a lot of enterprise analytics: the signal is scattered across multiple sources (FIFA rankings, historical results, form data, injury news), the volume is manageable, but the synthesis is slow and manual. A pundit watches twelve matches and synthesises an opinion over a weekend. An enterprise analyst runs a spreadsheet for three days. Neither has time to be wrong gracefully.

The interesting thing about SAP's RPT model is that it's designed for exactly this situation. Not "you have ten million rows and six months to train a model," but "you have a few hundred historical rows, the problem changes faster than your retraining schedule, and you need a prediction now." Football match history one World Cup every four years is precisely this kind of sparse, high-stakes dataset.

The key idea: RPT is an in-context learner for tabular data. You don't train it on your problem you hand it a set of historical rows and ask it to reason from them directly at inference time. Think of it as the world's most structured K-NN that actually read the footnotes. No gradient descent. No MLflow experiment to babysit. No retraining cycle after Brazil's last-minute squad change. You swap the context rows, and the model adapts instantly.

For comparison: XGBoost would be the better choice if you had thousands of labelled rows and a data engineer with free time. For sparse, fast-moving data in an SAP ecosystem where you want predictions inside the trust boundary, sharing the same OAuth, resource groups, and audit trail as the rest of your AI Core workloads RPT earns its keep.
 
The Architecture: Two Scouts, One Verdict
The system has three layers, and the design philosophy is straightforward: **separate fast facts from slow context, and make the fast path resilient when the slow path fails.**

At the core sits an RPT agent that collects structured match statistics FIFA rankings, goals averages, form scores, head-to-head records and calls the RPT model on SAP AI Core. This is the fast path. It returns a match outcome (W/L/D), goals estimate, and a confidence score per target in a few seconds.

Running in parallel on a separate Python thread is a news agent that calls a research LLM (via the SAP AI Core orchestration deployment) to find injuries, suspensions, and pre-match psychology. This is the enrichment path. It's valuable, but it's also occasionally slow like the pundit who went to check social media for injury news and came back fifteen minutes later with strong opinions and no sources. So it runs with a hard 120-second timeout. If it finishes in time, great. If not, the prediction completes on RPT alone and the final verdict explicitly says "no news context available."

The final layer is a synthesis LLM call that reads both results and writes a human-readable verdict: `SCORE / CONFIDENCE / REASONS`. Three lines. Clear enough for a football fan, honest enough for an engineer.

What's deliberately not in this architecture: a LangGraph agent loop for orchestration. An early version used one until I noticed I was burning one entire LLM round-trip just to decide which of two deterministic tasks to run next. Python's `ThreadPoolExecutor` does the same job faster and gives you hard control over the news timeout. Sometimes the right tool is a ten-line concurrent.futures block, not an agentic reasoning loop.
 
The Data: One Match, One Row, No Exceptions
The context rows come from StatsBomb open data publicly available match statistics covering FIFA World Cups and major international tournaments. Each historical match becomes one row, always from a single team's perspective.

That "single perspective" rule tripped me up early. If you include both "Brazil vs France (Brazil perspective)" and "France vs Brazil (France perspective)" from the same game, you've added noise, not information. The feature vectors are mirror images. RPT's similarity search pulls in the same game twice, double-weights it, and produces inconsistent context. One match, one row enforced by a pre-flight check that returns a descriptive error back to the agent for self-correction before anything reaches the API.

For teams not covered by StatsBomb (smaller federations, newer sides), the data agent estimates values from FIFA ranking history and flags those rows explicitly in the output. You'll see this in the demo result below and the model's honesty about its own data quality turned out to be one of the most useful parts of the whole system.
 
The SAP AI Core Deployment Gotcha
Before the demo, a quick note on a routing issue that cost me an afternoon and might save you one.

SAP AI Core distinguishes between two deployment types: `foundation-models` and `orchestration`. LiteLLM's `sap/` model prefix talks exclusively to orchestration deployments it calls `/lm/deployments` and filters for `scenarioId == "orchestration"`. If your deployment is `foundation-models`, the filtered list comes back empty and you get a cryptic `list index out of range` deep inside LiteLLM's transformation module. Not the error message you'd write if you were trying to help someone.

The fix is to switch to the `openai/` prefix with your deployment URL set as `api_base`. LiteLLM then routes through its OpenAI-compatible handler instead, which is perfectly happy talking to either deployment type. It's essentially the airport gate analogy: you got on the right plane, but you were standing at the wrong gate and the gate agent wasn't going to tell you where the plane actually was.

Check your deployment's `scenarioId` in SAP AI Launchpad before choosing your prefix. It's a two-minute check that prevents a two-hour debug session.
 
What It Actually Looks Like
Here is the detailed reference for the setup and what was the thought behind the Architecture design.
https://medium.com/@sabarna17/sap-rpt-and-llm-agents-for-world-cup-2026-football-match-predictions-8ccfa72fa20c
 
 
What This Means Beyond Football
The football scenario is deliberately concrete, but the architecture pattern generalises directly to enterprise use cases:
Maintenance order triage: RPT predicts criticality based on historical order patterns; an LLM agent pulls recent incident notes; synthesis produces a priority score with reasons.
Invoice exception handling: structured approval history as RPT context; free-text comments as LLM enrichment; parallel execution with a timeout on the enrichment path.
Sales forecasting: tabular history through RPT; market news and product launch context through a research model; same 'CONFIDENCE: HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW' output pattern.

The common thread is sparse, fast-moving tabular data paired with unstructured context which describes a substantial fraction of what SAP customers actually build on BTP.
 
Final Thoughts
What I didn't expect was how much of the project's value would come from the parts designed to handle failure well: the news timeout, the data quality flags, the explicit `MEDIUM` confidence. In production systems, graceful degradation isn't a nice-to-have. It's what determines whether your users trust the output at all.

If you're working on similar problems tabular prediction on sparse enterprise data, or parallel agent patterns on SAP AI Core I'd love to hear what you're building. Comments are open, and I promise to respond faster than a 120-second timeout.
 
One more thing, the RPT `COLUMNS` list is the contract between the data agent and the model. The first time I added a feature, I had to regenerate the entire context dataset. Treat it like a database migration, not a Python constant.

entry.title: Coffee Sales Predicted Everyday with SAP RPT
entry.content:
It Started with a Monday Morning Spreadsheet

It was a Monday morning. The kind where you arrive at the office before anyone else, stare at last week's sales numbers, and try to figure out how much stock to order for the week ahead. Our coffee shop manager, Priya, had been doing this for three years. Every Monday. Same spreadsheet. Same gut feeling. Same 20% over-ordering "just to be safe."

Priya knew the patterns intuitively. Hot days meant more iced lattes. Weekends meant brunch crowds. A promotion running meant foot traffic converted better. But turning that intuition into a number — a real, defensible forecast — required either a data scientist or a lot of luck. 

Then a colleague mentioned SAP RPT. "It's a foundation model for tabular data," he said. "You just send it your historical data and it predicts. No training. No model deployment. No PhD required."

Priya was sceptical. So was I. So we tested it. As I come from a technical background, building a quick prototype was quite easy. This document is the complete record of what happened, every step, every line of code, every result. By the end, you will have everything you need to run your own first prediction.

 

What you need to follow along?

1. An SAP BTP subaccount,

2. Python 3.9+ with pip, and

3. About 30 mins to 1 hour for the one-time BTP setup.

After that, each new prediction use case takes mere seconds to minutes

 

Part 1: Setting Up SAP BTP and AI Core (One-Time)

This setup is done once. Every prediction use case you build after this reuses the same service key and deployment ID.

Step 1: Log in to SAP BTP Cockpit

Navigate to https://cockpit.btp.cloud.sap and sign in.

  • Land on the Global Account overview
  • Click into your Subaccount (or create one — choose eu10 or us10 for AI Core support)

Note your Subaccount ID — you will need it for the service key

SAP BTP Global Account

Step 2: Subscribe to SAP AI Core (Extended Plan)

In your Subaccount: Services > Service Marketplace > search "SAP AI Core".

  1. Click the SAP AI Core tile
  2. Click "Create" — select the Extended plan (required for RPT foundation models)
  3. Wait 2-5 minutes for activation
  4. Verify: Services > Instances and Subscriptions shows SAP AI Core as "Subscribed"

Extended plan is mandatory.  The Free and Standard plans do not include foundation models. RPT is only available under the Extended plan. Check current plan pricing at: https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/serviceCatalog/sap-ai-core

SAP AI Core - Extended Plan

 

Step 3: Create a Service Key

The service key is a JSON file containing your OAuth credentials. Your Python code uses these to authenticate with the AI Core API.

  1. Services > Instances and Subscriptions > find your AI Core instance
  2. Click the three-dot menu > "Create Service Key"
  3. Name it (e.g., rpt-key) and click Create
  4. Click the key name > View > copy the entire JSON

The JSON structure you will receive:

{
  "clientid":     "sb-xxxxxxxx!bXXX|aicore!bXXX",
  "clientsecret": "xxxxxxxx$xxxxxxxx=",
  "url":          "https://<subdomain>.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com",
  "serviceurls": {
    "AI_API_URL": "https://api.ai.prod.eu-central-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com"
  }
}

 

Step 4: Find Your RPT Deployment ID

The Deployment ID identifies the running RPT model instance. SAP manages the deployment — you just need its ID.

  1. From Instances and Subscriptions, click "SAP AI Launchpad" to open it
  2. In AI Launchpad: ML Operations > Deployments
  3. Find a deployment with Scenario = "foundation-models" and Status = "Running"
  4. Copy the Deployment ID (16-character hex, e.g., dbff87ddae58361d)

No deployments visible?  Go to ML Operations > Configurations > Create a new configuration for the "foundation-models" scenario. Then deploy it from ML Operations > Deployments > Create. Takes about 5 minutes.

Select RPT model from Model Library in SAP AI Launchpad

 

Access the Deployment URL

 

Part 2: The Use Case — Coffee Shop Daily Sales

We predict daily coffee shop revenue from four observable features. This use case was chosen because the relationships are intuitive: hot days drive iced drink sales, weekends bring leisure customers, promotions convert foot traffic. You can sanity-check the predictions by eye.

Feature

Type

Description

Example

temp_f

Numeric

Outside temperature in degrees Fahrenheit

74

is_weekend

Binary (0/1)

1 = Saturday or Sunday, 0 = weekday

1

promotions

Integer

Number of active deals or discount offers

2

foot_traffic

Integer

Estimated pedestrians passing the shop

850

daily_sales

Numeric — TARGET

Total revenue in USD for the day

1,250

 We have 35 days of historical data covering different seasons, temperatures, and promotion levels. We want to predict sales for 5 upcoming days. The test rows have daily_sales = "[Predict]" — the sentinel value that tells RPT which rows to fill in.

 

Part 3: Setting Up Jupyter Notebook

All the code in this guide runs inside a Jupyter Notebook. Jupyter lets you run Python code cell by cell, see outputs immediately, and keep your credentials and results in one place. If you already have Jupyter installed, skip to Step 5.

Install Jupyter

Open a terminal (macOS/Linux) or Command Prompt (Windows) and run:

pip install jupyter notebook

# Or if you use conda:
conda install -c conda-forge notebook

Launch Jupyter Notebook

  1. In your terminal, navigate to your project folder:
cd ~/projects/sap-rpt-demo
  1. Start the Jupyter server:
jupyter notebook

Your browser will open automatically at http://localhost:8888. If it does not, copy the URL shown in the terminal (it includes a token).

Create a New Notebook

  1. Click "New" in the top-right corner
  2. Select "Python 3 (ipykernel)"
  3. A new notebook opens with an empty cell — you are ready to code
  4. Rename the notebook: click "Untitled" at the top and type "SAP_RPT_CoffeeShop"

How Jupyter cells work:  Each grey box is a cell. Type code into a cell and press Shift+Enter to run it. The output appears directly below. The cell number (e.g., In [1]:) increments each time you run a cell. All variables persist across cells in the same session.

 

Part 4: The Python Code — Each Cell in Your Notebook

Copy each cell below into your Jupyter notebook in order. Run each cell with Shift+Enter before moving to the next. The In [N]: header shows the cell number and the Out [N]: line shows the output.

Cell 1: Install Dependencies

In [1]:
import sys
!{sys.executable} -m pip install requests pandas --quiet
import requests, pandas as pd, json
print('Libraries ready.')
Out [1]: Libraries ready.

Security note:  Never commit credentials to source control. Use environment variables or a secrets manager. In production: DEPLOYMENT_ID = os.environ["RPT_DEPLOYMENT_ID"]

Cell 2: Configure Credentials

Paste the values from your BTP service key (Step 3) and deployment ID (Step 4):

In [2]:
# Paste from your BTP service key
AI_CORE_AUTH_URL      = 'https://<subdomain>.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com'
AI_CORE_CLIENT_ID     = 'sb-<uuid>!b<n>|aicore!b<n>'
AI_CORE_CLIENT_SECRET = '<uuid>$<secret>='
AI_CORE_API_BASE      = 'https://api.ai.prod.eu-central-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com'
DEPLOYMENT_ID         = '<your-16-char-id>'
RESOURCE_GROUP        = 'default'
print('Configuration loaded.')
Out [2]: Configuration loaded.

Cell 3: Get an OAuth Token

SAP AI Core uses OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials. The token is valid for 12 hours. Cache it and reuse it — do not request a new token per prediction call.

In [3]:	
r = requests.post(
    f'{AI_CORE_AUTH_URL}/oauth/token',
    data={'grant_type': 'client_credentials'},
    auth=(AI_CORE_CLIENT_ID, AI_CORE_CLIENT_SECRET),
    timeout=30
)
r.raise_for_status()
TOKEN = r.json()['access_token']
print(f'Token valid for {r.json()["expires_in"] // 3600} hours.')
Out [3]: Token valid for 12 hours.

Cell 4: Build the Combined Dataset

The combined dataset pattern is the core of how RPT works. Training rows (with real labels) and test rows (with "[Predict]") are sent together in a single list. RPT learns from the labelled rows and fills in the placeholder rows. We use 35 training days covering cold winter days, mild spring days, hot summer days, and everything in between.

In [4]:
TARGET      = 'daily_sales'
PLACEHOLDER = '[Predict]'
COLS        = ['temp_f', 'is_weekend', 'promotions', 'foot_traffic']
ALL_COLS    = COLS + [TARGET]

# 35 days of historical data (varied seasons and conditions)
train_features = [
    # Mild weekdays
    [68,0,1,620],[72,0,1,650],[65,0,0,580],[70,0,1,610],[67,0,0,590],
    [71,0,1,630],[69,0,0,600],[73,0,2,660],[66,0,1,570],[64,0,0,550],
    # Warm/hot weekdays
    [80,0,2,720],[85,0,2,750],[88,0,1,700],[90,0,3,780],[92,0,2,760],
    [95,0,3,800],[83,0,1,710],[87,0,2,740],[84,0,0,690],[89,0,2,770],
    # Weekends (mild to hot)
    [75,1,2,920],[80,1,2,980],[85,1,3,1050],[88,1,3,1100],[78,1,2,950],
    [82,1,2,990],[90,1,3,1150],[92,1,3,1200],[76,1,2,930],[83,1,2,1000],
    # Cold days
    [45,0,0,420],[48,0,1,450],[50,1,1,580],[52,1,2,620],[55,0,1,500],
]
train_labels = [
    # Mild weekdays
    820,870,760,800,790,850,780,890,750,720,
    # Warm/hot weekdays
    950,1020,980,1080,1050,1120,960,1010,940,1060,
    # Weekends
    1150,1220,1320,1380,1180,1250,1450,1500,1160,1280,
    # Cold days
    560,590,720,780,650,
]
# 5 upcoming days to predict
test_features = [
    [74,0,1,640],    # Wednesday, mild, 1 promo
    [86,1,2,1020],   # Saturday, hot, 2 promos
    [91,1,3,1180],   # Sunday, very hot, 3 promos
    [47,0,0,430],    # Cold Tuesday, no promo
    [79,1,3,1050],   # Warm Saturday, 3 promos
]
all_records = (
    [dict(zip(ALL_COLS, f+[l])) for f,l in zip(train_features,train_labels)] +
    [dict(zip(ALL_COLS, f+[PLACEHOLDER])) for f in test_features]
)
print(f'Dataset: {len(all_records)} rows ({len(train_features)} train + {len(test_features)} predict)')
Out [4]: Dataset: 40 rows (35 train + 5 predict)

Cell 5: Call the RPT API

The payload schema is strict. The prediction_placeholder value in target_columns must exactly match the value used in the rows.

In [5]:
url  = f'{AI_CORE_API_BASE}/v2/inference/deployments/{DEPLOYMENT_ID}/predict'
hdrs = {
    'Authorization':     f'Bearer {TOKEN}',
    'AI-Resource-Group': RESOURCE_GROUP,
    'Content-Type':      'application/json'
}
payload = {
    'prediction_config': {
        'target_columns': [
            {'name': TARGET, 'prediction_placeholder': PLACEHOLDER}
        ]
    },
    'rows': all_records
}
resp = requests.post(url, headers=hdrs, json=payload, timeout=60)
print(f'HTTP {resp.status_code}', 'OK' if resp.status_code == 200 else resp.text[:200])
Out [5]: HTTP 200 OK

Cell 6: Parse the Response

The RPT response nests predictions four levels deep: outer dict > list > inner dict > "prediction" key. The prediction value is a string — cast it to float.

In [6]:
preds_raw = resp.json()['predictions']

def to_scalar(v):
    if isinstance(v, dict): v = v.get(TARGET, list(v.values())[0])
    while isinstance(v, (list, tuple)) and len(v) > 0: v = v[0]
    if isinstance(v, dict): v = v.get('prediction', list(v.values())[0])
    return float(v)

def to_confidence(v):
    if isinstance(v, dict): v = v.get(TARGET, list(v.values())[0])
    while isinstance(v, (list, tuple)) and len(v) > 0: v = v[0]
    return float(v.get('confidence', 0)) if isinstance(v, dict) else 0.0

preds = [to_scalar(p) for p in preds_raw][-len(test_features):]
confs = [to_confidence(p) for p in preds_raw][-len(test_features):]

days = [
    'Wednesday (74F, mild, 1 promo)',
    'Saturday  (86F, hot, 2 promos)',
    'Sunday    (91F, very hot, 3 promos)',
    'Tuesday   (47F, cold, no promo)',
    'Saturday  (79F, warm, 3 promos)',
]
print('=== RPT Predictions ===')
for day, pred, conf in zip(days, preds, confs):
    print(f'{day:<40} -> ${pred:>7,.0f}   confidence={conf:.2f}')
Out [6]: === RPT Predictions ===
Wednesday (74F, mild, 1 promo)           ->    $870   confidence=0.74
Saturday  (86F, hot, 2 promos)           ->  $1,290   confidence=0.86
Sunday    (91F, very hot, 3 promos)      ->  $1,490   confidence=0.92
Tuesday   (47F, cold, no promo)          ->    $575   confidence=0.81
Saturday  (79F, warm, 3 promos)          ->  $1,180   confidence=0.88

 

Part 4: Results and What They Tell Us

Running the code above against a live SAP AI Core RPT deployment produces the following predictions:

Day

Temp (F)

Weekend

Promos

Foot Traffic

RPT Prediction

Confidence

Wednesday

74

No

1

640

$870

0.74

Saturday

86

Yes

2

1,020

$1,290

0.86

Sunday

91

Yes

3

1,180

$1,490

0.92

Tuesday

47

No

0

430

$575

0.81

Saturday

79

Yes

3

1,050

$1,180

0.88

The predictions are directionally correct and internally consistent. Sunday (hottest, most promotions, highest foot traffic, weekend) gets the highest prediction. Cold Tuesday (no promotion, low foot traffic) gets the lowest. The confidence scores reflect pattern strength — Sunday at 0.92 closely matches many similar hot weekend days in the training data, while Wednesday at 0.74 is a more average day with fewer strong signals.

 

Part 5: Full Sample Data Table — All 40 Rows

The table below shows every row sent to the RPT API. Rows 1-35 are training data with known sales values covering mild weekdays, warm/hot weekdays, weekends, and cold days. Rows 36-40 had daily_sales = "[Predict]" in the request. The green values are what RPT returned.

#

promotions

Rows 1-35 are training data with actual sales values covering four weather and day-type categories. Rows 36-40 were sent with daily_sales = "[Predict]". The green values are what RPT returned, along with confidence scores.

Conclusion: From Zero to Production in One Afternoon

Priya ran the code on a Tuesday morning. By the time her second coffee was ready, she had predictions for the next five days — with confidence scores telling her how certain the model was. The Sunday prediction came back at $1,490 with 0.92 confidence. She ordered accordingly. She was right within 4%.

That is the promise of SAP RPT. Not magic. Not a replacement for human judgment. But a fast, cheap, accurate starting point that takes hours instead of months to set up.

Here is what you accomplished in this guide:

  1. Set up SAP BTP and subscribed to AI Core Extended plan
  2. Created a service key and obtained OAuth credentials
  3. Located the RPT deployment ID in AI Launchpad
  4. Set up Jupyter Notebook and organised all code into runnable cells
  5. Built a 40-row combined dataset with "[Predict]" placeholders
  6. Called the RPT API and received predictions with confidence scores
  7. Parsed the four-level nested response structure

The coffee shop example is intentionally simple. The same code with different column names and different data works for sales forecasting, customer churn prediction, demand planning, fraud scoring, equipment failure prediction, and any other tabular regression or classification task your organisation needs.

I would love your feedback!  Did this guide help you get your first RPT prediction running? Was anything unclear, missing, or could be explained better? Drop a comment below or reach out directly, trust me, your feedback shapes future guides. Even a quick "worked for me" or "stuck at Step X" is incredibly helpful. Thank you for reading.

entry.title: Getting Started with SAP Snowflake in Business Data Cloud: Build Your First ML Model
entry.content:
With the recently announced partnership, Snowflake is now available as a Solution Extension inside SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC). Together with existing offerings such as SAP HANA Cloud ML capabilities (PAL/APL), SAP Databricks, and the RPT-1.5 model, it represents another major building block in SAP's growing AI, machine learning, and data science ecosystem. This unlocks a powerful combination: your trusted SAP business data on one side, and Snowflake's AI Data Cloud with its rich ML and Cortex AI capabilities on the other - all governed, integrated, and without the traditional ETL headaches.

In this hands-on getting-started guide, we'll walk through your very first end-to-end ML project on Snowflake through BDC. We'll:

  1. Access Snowflake through SAP BDC
  2. Manually upload the classic Iris dataset
  3. Create a Snowflake Notebook and explore its Markdown, SQL, and Python cell types
  4. Use the Cortex AI Copilot to generate a new cell for us
  5. Train a simple Random Forest classifier using snowflake-ml-python
  6. Visualize the results - all without leaving the notebook

Before we start, I'd like to thank @Tobias_Mueller and @AndreasForster for their support with this blog. Now, let's dive in.

Table of contents

  • The Use Case
  • Architecture
  • Prerequisites
  • Step 1 - Access Snowflake from SAP Business Data Cloud
  • Step 2 - Set Up Warehouse, Database and Schema
  • Step 3 - Upload the Iris CSV Manually
  • Step 4 - Create a Snowflake Notebook
  • Step 5 - Explore the Three Cell Types
  • Step 6 - Generate a Cell Using Cortex AI Copilot
  • Step 7 - Verify Packages & Train a Random Forest with Snowflake ML
  • Step 8 - Show the Results (Right Inside the Notebook)
  • Step 9 - What Else You Should Know
  • Conclusion & Next Steps

The Use Case

For this getting-started guide, we'll use the classic Iris flower dataset - a small, clean dataset of 150 flower samples, each described by four measurements (sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width) and labeled with one of three species: setosa, versicolor, or virginica. Our goal is a simple multi-class classification problem: predict the species of a flower from its measurements.

Why Iris? It's the "Hello World" of machine learning. Small enough to train in seconds, yet rich enough to demonstrate a complete end-to-end ML workflow: data ingestion, exploration, model training, and evaluation. This lets us focus on how the SAP BDC × Snowflake platform works rather than on data wrangling.

But think of Iris as a stand-in for your real SAP business data. The exact same pattern - take structured records, engineer features, train a classifier, and score new data natively in Snowflake - maps directly onto high-value enterprise scenarios, for example:

      • Customer churn prediction on CRM / Sales Cloud data
      • Demand forecasting on S/4HANA sales and inventory data
      • Employee attrition modeling on SuccessFactors data
      • Invoice or expense anomaly detection on Ariba / finance data

In each case, the four "petal and sepal measurements" simply become your business features (e.g., contract length, order frequency, tenure), and the "species" becomes your business outcome (e.g., churn / no-churn). Once you've completed this walkthrough with Iris, you'll have the blueprint to swap in genuine SAP data products and derive real insights - without moving data out of your governed landscape.

Architecture

Before we get hands-on, let's understand where Snowflake fits within the SAP Business Data Cloud landscape.

The Big Picture: Snowflake as a BDC Solution Extension

At the highest level, SAP Business Data Cloud unifies your SAP data sources (S/4HANA, HCM, SuccessFactors, Concur, and more) into a governed Business Data Fabric. SAP Snowflake plugs in as a Solution Extension, bringing Zero-Copy data access and Custom AI/ML capabilities right alongside SAP BW PCE, the BDC Cockpit, and SAP Datasphere - all consuming from a shared object store of SAP and custom data products.

The End-to-End Flow: From SAP Data to ML Insights

In a full production scenario, the journey of data looks like this: SAP data products are defined at the source (e.g., S/4HANA PCE), exposed through the SAP Foundation Service, and shared into the BDC Cockpit / SAP Datasphere Data Catalog. From there, they flow zero-copy into the SAP Snowflake Unity/Horizon Catalog, where a Notebook trains a model and produces an ML Dataset. That dataset can then be shared back to SAP Datasphere and consumed by analytic models, views, or a story in SAP Analytics Cloud - closing the loop from raw SAP data to actionable ML insight.

 

Our Focus for This Guide

That end-to-end flow is powerful, but it involves several SAP components working together. For this getting-started guide, we'll keep things simple and stay entirely within SAP Snowflake. Uploading data manually and training a model in a notebook so we can focus purely on learning the tool itself. Once you're comfortable here, the zero-copy integration with the rest of BDC is the natural next step.

 

Prerequisites

      • Access to SAP Business Data Cloud with the Snowflake Solution Extension enabled
      • A Snowflake account/warehouse provisioned through BDC
      • Basic knowledge of SQL and Python
      • The Iris dataset CSV (download from this GitHub gist)

Step 1 - Access Snowflake from SAP Business Data Cloud

From your SAP BDC launchpad, navigate to the Snowflake Solution Extension tile. This opens Snowflake's Snowsight UI, already authenticated with your SAP identity. No separate login, no context switching.

 

💡The Snowflake environment provisioned through BDC is a fully managed Snowflake account, but with governance and identity tied back to your SAP landscape.

Step 2 - Set Up Warehouse, Database and Schema

Instead of using SQL commands, we'll create our Snowflake objects through the intuitive Snowsight UI.

Create a Warehouse

      1. In Snowsight, navigate to Home.
      2. Click + Create Warehouse.
      3. Configure your warehouse:
        • Name: ML_DEMO_WH
        • Size: X-Small (recommended for this demo)
        • Auto Suspend: 60 minutes
        • Auto Resume: Enabled (default)
      4. Click Create Warehouse.

Create a Database and Schema

      1. Navigate to Catalog → Database Explorer (or Data → Databases).
      2. Click + Database and enter the name ML_DEMO_DB, then click Create.
      3. Open your new ML_DEMO_DB database, click + Schema, enter the name IRIS, and click Create.

 

Step 3 - Upload the Iris CSV Manually

Now let's get our training data into Snowflake. No scripts, just clicks. There are two ways to reach the data loading wizard:

      • Option A: From Home, click + Upload local files.
      • Option B: Navigate to Ingestion → Add Data → Load data into a table.

Either path opens the same load-data wizard. From there:

      1. Select the ML_DEMO_WH warehouse on the top right of the pop-up window
      2. Choose the ML_DEMO_DB.IRIS schema and enter a new table name: IRIS_DATA.
      3. Drag and drop your iris.csv file into the wizard.
      4. Snowflake will auto-detect the schema. Confirm the column types (four NUMBER features and one VARCHAR species column).
      5. Click Load.

 

You can verify the upload by clicking on View table details. Here you'll see a preview of your data, can check out the Data Quality or Table Details and explore the Lineage of the IRIS_DATA table.

 

Step 4 - Create a Snowflake Notebook

Navigate to Projects → Workspaces (My Workspace) → + Add new → Notebook.

      • Name: iris_ml_demo
      • Location: ML_DEMO_DB.IRIS
      • Connected Service: Use a Service with Python 3.12 v2.3, a compute pool with at least CPU_X64_S (ideal for hosting multiple services/jobs while saving cost) & Artifact SNOWPARK
      • Warehouse: ML_DEMO_WH

 

Step 5 - Explore the Three Cell Types

Snowflake Notebooks support three different cell types. You can add the one you need by clicking the corresponding button at the bottom of the notebook, or by inserting a new cell and changing its type using the dropdown in the top-left corner. Let's explore all three.

Markdown Cell

# Iris Classification with Snowflake ML
This notebook trains a Random Forest classifier on the Iris dataset,
using data stored natively in Snowflake and the `snowflake-ml-python` library.

SQL Cell

Name this cell & its output iris_sql (the name matters. We'll reference it from Python):

SELECT * FROM ML_DEMO_DB.IRIS.IRIS_DATA;

Add a second SQL cell for a quick sanity check:

SELECT species, 
       AVG(sepal_length) AS avg_sepal_length,
       AVG(petal_length) AS avg_petal_length
FROM ML_DEMO_DB.IRIS.IRIS_DATA
GROUP BY species;

Python Cell

Here's the magic. You can reference the SQL cell's result directly:

# Reference the SQL cell's result directly and make a working copy
df = iris_sql.copy()
print(df.shape)
df.head()

 

Step 6 - Generate a Cell Using Cortex AI Copilot

Snowflake Notebooks come with a built-in Cortex AI Copilot. Click the ✨ (Ask Copilot) icon in the sidebar to open the chat panel.

Try a prompt like:

"Generate a Python cell that plots the distribution of each numeric feature grouped by species using matplotlib."

Cortex will produce something like:

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

numeric_cols = ['SEPAL_LENGTH', 'SEPAL_WIDTH', 'PETAL_LENGTH', 'PETAL_WIDTH']
species_list = df['SPECIES'].unique()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 8))
axes = axes.flatten()

for i, col in enumerate(numeric_cols):
    for species in species_list:
        subset = df[df['SPECIES'] == species]
        axes[i].hist(subset[col], alpha=0.6, label=species, bins=15)
    axes[i].set_title(f'Distribution of {col}')
    axes[i].set_xlabel(col)
    axes[i].set_ylabel('Frequency')
    axes[i].legend()

plt.suptitle('Feature Distributions by Species', fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Click Keep all so the newly generated python cell is inserted in the Notebook. No copy-paste needed.

 

💡Try other prompts like "Split the data into train and test sets" or "Show me a correlation heatmap of the features".

Fix an Error with Cortex

Copilot doesn't just generate code - it can also fix it. To demonstrate this, we'll intentionally introduce a typo in the following Python cell. The misspelled describe() method (descibe) will trigger an AttributeError, allowing us to see how Cortex identifies and fixes the issue.

# Generate a statistical summary of the numeric features
numeric_cols = ['SEPAL_LENGTH', 'SEPAL_WIDTH', 'PETAL_LENGTH', 'PETAL_WIDTH']
summary = df[numeric_cols].descibe()
print(summary)

Run the cell to trigger the intentional AttributeError. Rather than debugging it manually, click the ✨Fix error button that appears alongside the error. Cortex reads the traceback, pinpoints the problem, and suggests the corrected code which you can apply with a single click.

 

Step 7 - Verify Packages & Train a Random Forest with Snowflake ML

You may have noticed we never had to install any packages. That's because Snowflake Notebooks come with ~100 of the most popular data-science and ML packages preinstalled, along with 1st-party Snowflake packages such as snowflake-snowpark-python.

Before we dive into training, let's quickly confirm the packages we need are available. This is just a check, not an import:

from importlib.metadata import version, PackageNotFoundError

for pkg in ["snowflake-ml-python", "pandas", "scikit-learn", "matplotlib"]:
    try:
        print(f"✅ {pkg}: {version(pkg)}")
    except PackageNotFoundError:
        print(f"❌ {pkg}: NOT available")

Now the fun part. The snowflake.ml.modeling library brings a scikit-learn–like API directly onto Snowflake, so training runs in Snowflake.

from snowflake.snowpark.context import get_active_session
from snowflake.ml.modeling.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

session = get_active_session()
session.sql("USE DATABASE ML_DEMO_DB").collect()
session.sql("USE SCHEMA IRIS").collect()

# Load data as a Snowpark DataFrame
snow_df = session.table("IRIS_DATA")

# Split into train and test
train_df, test_df = snow_df.random_split(weights=[0.8, 0.2], seed=42)

# Define feature columns and target
FEATURE_COLS = ["SEPAL_LENGTH", "SEPAL_WIDTH", "PETAL_LENGTH", "PETAL_WIDTH"]
LABEL_COL = "SPECIES"
OUTPUT_COL = "PREDICTED_SPECIES"

# Train the model
model = RandomForestClassifier(
    input_cols=FEATURE_COLS,
    label_cols=LABEL_COL,
    output_cols=OUTPUT_COL,
    n_estimators=100,
    random_state=42,
)
model.fit(train_df)

# Predict on the test set
predictions = model.predict(test_df)
predictions.select(FEATURE_COLS + [LABEL_COL, OUTPUT_COL]).show(10)

When you run this cell, Snowflake spins up the training in-database. After fit() completes, predict() appends a PREDICTED_SPECIES column, and the last line prints a 10-row sample so you can eyeball the model's predictions against the actual species.

Step 8 - Show the Results (Right Inside the Notebook)

Let's evaluate our model with three quick views:

from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, confusion_matrix
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

pred_pd = predictions.to_pandas()

# 1. Accuracy
acc = accuracy_score(pred_pd[LABEL_COL], pred_pd[OUTPUT_COL])
print(f"✅ Test accuracy: {acc:.3f}")

# 2. Confusion matrix
cm = pd.crosstab(pred_pd[LABEL_COL], pred_pd[OUTPUT_COL],
                 rownames=['Actual'], colnames=['Predicted'])
print("\nConfusion matrix:")
print(cm)

# 3. Feature importances
sk_model = model.to_sklearn()
importances = pd.Series(sk_model.feature_importances_, index=FEATURE_COLS)\
                .sort_values()

importances.plot(kind='barh', title='Feature Importances', figsize=(6, 3))
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

You'll typically see an accuracy around 0.93–1.00 on Iris, with petal length and petal width as the top features.

 

That's it! A full ML workflow, entirely inside Snowflake, launched from SAP Business Data Cloud.

Step 9 - What Else You Should Know

Before you go, here are two handy features tucked away in the notebook's Tools Panel at the very bottom of the screen. They are completely independent from each other.

Explore Cell Dependencies in the Tools Panel

Open the Tools Panel at the very bottom of the notebook and switch to the Dependencies tab. Here Snowflake visualizes how the cells throughout your notebook are linked with each other. For example, how our Python cell references the iris_sql result, and how later cells build on that DataFrame and the trained model. This dependency view helps you:

  • Understand the flow of data from one cell to the next at a glance.
  • See which cells depend on which, so you know exactly what needs to re-run when you change something upstream.
  • Trace and debug your notebook logic without manually scanning through every cell.

Schedule Notebooks with the Job Scheduler

The Job Scheduler is another capability in the notebook. With it, you can turn your notebook into a recurring job - set a schedule (e.g., hourly or daily), pick a warehouse, and let Snowflake run it automatically. This is perfect for repeatable training or reporting, with no external orchestrator needed.

 

Conclusion & Next Steps

In under 30 minutes, you've gone from "I have SAP BDC access" to "I have a trained ML model running natively in Snowflake." That's the power of the SAP × Snowflake partnership: less plumbing, more insights. While this tutorial focused on building and running ML directly in the SAP Snowflake environment, a production solution would typically continue by writing predictions or enriched datasets back to SAP Business Data Cloud, where they can be consumed across SAP applications.

Where to go next:

      • SAP Business Data Cloud, Snowflake edition
      • Zero-Copy integration between SAP and Snowflake
      • Snowflake ML documentation
      • Snowflake Cortex AI documentation

What's coming next in this series

This is just the beginning. I'm planning a series of follow-up blogs exploring more of what SAP Snowflake has to offer. Here's what's coming soon:

      • Building Streamlit Apps in Snowflake - using the built-in Streamlit functionality to create interactive data apps directly inside Snowflake, no separate hosting required.
      • From SAP-Managed to Custom-Managed Data Products - consuming SAP-managed Data Products, building your own custom-managed Data Products in SAP Snowflake, and sharing them back into the SAP landscape.
      • Bringing SAP Datasphere / HANA Cloud ML into SAP Snowflake - leveraging the ML capabilities of SAP Datasphere / HANA Cloud from within SAP Snowflake by allowing External Access Integration.

Connect with me on LinkedIn to stay up to date and get notified when the next blogs in this series go live.

Now it's your turn. What SAP data would you love to run an ML model on through Snowflake? Drop your ideas in the comments below.

Happy modeling! 🦾
